era_context: The year 1500 marks a pivotal moment in global history: the Portuguese have just completed the sea route to India; the Safavid movement is consolidating power in Persia; the Ottoman Empire dominates the Eastern Mediterranean; the Italian Renaissance is flourishing; and the Americas are on the cusp of sustained European contact. The Aztec and Inca empires remain at their territorial peaks, unaware of the impending arrival of European conquistadors.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; Portuguese coastal exploration not yet systematically documented)
Interior of the Americas (no European documentation; Aztec and Inca records fragmentary)
East Asia and Southeast Asia (limited European sources; Chinese and Southeast Asian records exist but are underrepresented in Western scholarship)

events.title: Pedro Álvares Cabral's expedition reaches India; discovery of Brazil
events.region: Atlantic Ocean and Indian Ocean; Brazil and India
events.description: Pedro Álvares Cabral, commanding a Portuguese fleet of 13 ships, sailed from Lisbon in March 1500 with the goal of reaching India via the Cape of Good Hope. Due to winds and currents (or possibly deliberate navigation), Cabral's fleet was blown westward and reached the coast of South America (modern Brazil) in April 1500, making the first documented European contact with the region. After exploring the Brazilian coast and establishing initial contact with indigenous peoples, Cabral continued to India, arriving in Calicut in September 1500. His expedition confirmed the viability of the sea route to India and brought back spices and news of the newly discovered lands. The voyage was a triumph of Portuguese maritime technology and navigation.

sources.name: Portuguese royal chronicles and letters, particularly the Cartas de El-Rei Dom Manuel I
Roteiros (pilot books) and navigational records
Pero Vaz de Caminha's letter describing the Brazilian coast and indigenous peoples
Historiographic consensus on Portuguese maritime expansion

events.certainty_note: Cabral's voyage is exceptionally well-documented in contemporary Portuguese sources, including the famous letter by Pero Vaz de Caminha. The dates (March departure, April arrival in Brazil, September arrival in India) are secure.
cross_references: 1501-002 (Portuguese establishment of trading posts in India)

events.title: Battle of Diu (First Battle of Diu); Portuguese naval victory in India
events.region: Arabian Sea, off the coast of Diu (modern Gujarat, India)
events.description: In September 1500, as Cabral's fleet approached India, a naval battle occurred off the coast of Diu between Portuguese ships and a combined fleet of Arab, Egyptian, and Indian vessels. The Portuguese, under Cabral's command, achieved a decisive victory, sinking or capturing several enemy ships. This battle demonstrated Portuguese naval superiority and established their military dominance in Indian Ocean waters. The victory facilitated Portuguese trading activities and the establishment of fortified posts along the Indian coast.
key_figures: Arab and Egyptian naval commanders (names not consistently recorded in Portuguese sources)

sources.name: Portuguese royal chronicles and Cabral's own reports
Pero Vaz de Caminha's letter
Later Portuguese historiography, particularly by João de Barros

events.certainty_note: The battle is well-attested in Portuguese sources. However, Arab and Indian sources provide limited contemporary documentation, so the battle is primarily known through Portuguese accounts, which may emphasize Portuguese achievements.
cross_references: 1509-001 (Battle of Diu, 1509, a later and more famous battle)

events.title: Death of John of Gaunt's line; succession crisis in England (Henry VII reigning)
events.description: In 1500, King Henry VII of England (r. 1485–1509) was consolidating his dynasty after the Wars of the Roses. The year marked a period of relative stability in England, with Henry VII strengthening his position through strategic marriages and alliances. His son Arthur, Prince of Wales, was alive and healthy, ensuring the succession. However, the broader context of 1500 in England was one of careful political management, as Henry VII worked to prevent rival claimants from challenging his rule. The year itself saw no major political upheaval, but it was part of the broader process of establishing the Tudor dynasty.

sources.name: Tudor court records and chronicles
Later English historiography, particularly by Edward Hall and Raphael Holinshed
Historiographic consensus on Tudor England

events.certainty_note: The political situation in England in 1500 is well-documented in court records. However, 1500 itself was not a year of major events; it was a period of consolidation.
cross_references: 1502-001 (Death of Arthur, Prince of Wales, succession crisis)

events.title: Zheng He's treasure voyages continue; Ming Dynasty maritime expeditions
events.region: Indian Ocean, Southeast Asia, East Africa
events.description: In 1500, the Ming Dynasty of China was in the midst of its great maritime expeditions under the command of the eunuch admiral Zheng He. Zheng He's treasure voyages (1405–1433) were among the largest and most sophisticated maritime expeditions of the pre-modern world, involving massive treasure ships and fleets that explored the Indian Ocean, visited East Africa, and established Chinese diplomatic and trade networks. By 1500, these voyages were in their later stages, but they represented the height of Chinese maritime power and technological achievement. The voyages demonstrated Chinese naval superiority and the sophistication of Ming Dynasty navigation and shipbuilding.
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (r. 1402–1424, patron of earlier voyages)
Hongwu Emperor's successors (patrons of later voyages)

sources.name: Ming Dynasty court records and the Mingshi (History of the Ming Dynasty)
The Yingya Shenglan (Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores) by Ma Huan, a participant in Zheng He's voyages
Archaeological evidence from shipwrecks and coastal sites

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's voyages are well-documented in Chinese sources. However, the exact dates and details of individual voyages are sometimes uncertain, and the voyages were largely abandoned after 1433 due to political changes in the Ming court. By 1500, the voyages had ended, though their legacy remained.

events.title: Montezuma II becomes Aztec Emperor
events.description: In 1502 (or possibly 1500, depending on the source), Montezuma II (also known as Moctezuma II) became the ninth emperor of the Aztec Empire, succeeding his uncle Ahuitzotl. Montezuma II would rule until his death in 1520, during which time he expanded Aztec territory and consolidated imperial power. However, his reign would be cut short by the arrival of Spanish conquistadors under Hernán Cortés in 1519. The year 1500 marks the beginning of a new era in Aztec history, though Montezuma II's succession is sometimes dated to 1502. Montezuma II is remembered as a complex figure: a capable military commander and administrator, but also a ruler whose indecision and superstitious beliefs may have contributed to the fall of the Aztec Empire.

sources.name: Aztec codices, particularly the Codex Mendoza and the Florentine Codex
Spanish chronicles by Bernal Díaz del Castillo and others
Nahuatl-language sources compiled after the Spanish conquest

events.certainty_note: The dating of Montezuma II's succession is uncertain. Most sources place it in 1502, but some Aztec sources suggest 1500. The Aztec calendar system and the conversion to the Gregorian calendar introduce uncertainty. The year 1500 is used here as an approximate date, but 1502 is more commonly cited in modern scholarship.
cross_references: 1519-001 (Arrival of Hernán Cortés and the Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire)

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Cabral's 'discovery' of Brazil is sometimes overstated; indigenous peoples had inhabited the region for thousands of years, and the Portuguese arrival was not a discovery but a first contact from a European perspective. Additionally, the extent of Cabral's deliberate navigation versus accidental drift to Brazil remains debated among historians. Some scholars argue that the Portuguese were already aware of land to the west and deliberately sailed to investigate, while others maintain that the westward drift was unintentional. The Battle of Diu in 1500 is sometimes confused with the more famous Battle of Diu in 1509; the 1500 battle is less well-documented in non-Portuguese sources. Regarding Montezuma II's succession, the dating is genuinely uncertain, and the year 1500 may be incorrect; 1502 is more widely accepted in modern scholarship.
historiographic_note: The year 1500 is exceptionally well-documented for European history, particularly Portuguese maritime exploration, which is extensively recorded in royal archives and contemporary accounts. However, the documentation is heavily weighted toward European perspectives and interests. For the Americas, documentation is sparse and comes primarily from Spanish sources written after the conquest; indigenous Aztec and Inca records are fragmentary and were often compiled after European contact. For Asia, Chinese sources are abundant but are underrepresented in Western scholarship; the Ming Dynasty's maritime activities are well-documented in Chinese archives but less familiar to Western historians. African and Southeast Asian perspectives are largely absent from surviving written records accessible to modern scholarship. The year 1500 is often treated as a symbolic turning point in world history (the 'discovery' of the Americas, the beginning of European global dominance), but this periodization reflects a Eurocentric bias and obscures the continued vitality and sophistication of non-European civilizations at this time.

graph_edges.note: Cabral's successful voyage to India in 1500 led to the establishment of Portuguese trading posts in 1501.
The 1500 Battle of Diu established Portuguese naval dominance, leading to further Portuguese expansion and the more famous Battle of Diu in 1509.
Zheng He's voyages were largely complete by 1500, having ended around 1433.
Montezuma II's rule (beginning 1500 or 1502) ended with the arrival of Spanish conquistadors in 1519.

era_context: The late 15th century: the Portuguese maritime empire is on the cusp of establishing dominance in the Indian Ocean; Vasco da Gama has just returned from his pioneering voyage to India; the Ottoman Empire controls the Eastern Mediterranean; the Italian Renaissance is in full flourish; and the Americas remain unknown to Eurasian powers. The Aztec and Inca empires are at their territorial peaks.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; Portuguese coastal exploration not yet systematically documented)
Interior of the Americas (no European documentation; Aztec and Inca records fragmentary)
East Asia and Southeast Asia (limited European sources; Chinese and Southeast Asian records exist but are underrepresented in Western scholarship)

events.title: Vasco da Gama returns to Portugal from India; confirmation of the sea route
events.region: Atlantic Ocean and Indian Ocean; Portugal and India
events.description: Vasco da Gama, commanding a Portuguese fleet of four ships, returned to Lisbon in September 1499 after completing the first European sea voyage to India (1497–1499). Da Gama's expedition had sailed around the Cape of Good Hope, crossed the Indian Ocean, and reached Calicut (modern Kozhikode) in May 1498. The return voyage was arduous, with significant loss of life due to disease and storms, but da Gama brought back spices and news of the successful route. His return confirmed the viability of the sea route to India and demonstrated Portuguese maritime superiority. The voyage was a triumph of Portuguese navigation, shipbuilding, and determination, and it fundamentally altered European understanding of global geography and trade routes.

sources.name: Portuguese royal chronicles and letters
Roteiros (pilot books) and navigational records
Later Portuguese historiography, particularly by João de Barros

events.certainty_note: Da Gama's return in 1499 is exceptionally well-documented in Portuguese sources. The dates of his voyage (departure 1497, arrival in India 1498, return 1499) are secure.

events.title: First Venetian-Ottoman War begins; Ottoman expansion in the Mediterranean
events.region: Eastern Mediterranean, Aegean Sea, Ottoman Empire and Venice
events.description: In 1499, tensions between the Ottoman Empire and the Venetian Republic escalated into open warfare. The First Venetian-Ottoman War (1499–1503) was fought over control of Mediterranean trade routes and Venetian-held territories in the Aegean and Ionian seas. Ottoman forces, under the command of Sultan Bayezid II and Admiral Kemal Reis, conducted naval raids and sieges against Venetian positions. The war demonstrated Ottoman naval power and the decline of Venetian dominance in the Mediterranean. Venice, despite its wealth and naval tradition, was unable to match Ottoman military resources and eventually ceded several territories to the Ottomans.

sources.name: Ottoman chronicles, particularly those compiled during Bayezid II's reign
Venetian state records and diplomatic correspondence
Contemporary accounts by Venetian and Ottoman observers

events.certainty_note: The First Venetian-Ottoman War is well-documented in both Ottoman and Venetian sources. The year 1499 is consistently cited as the beginning of the war.

events.title: Amerigo Vespucci's second voyage to the Americas
events.region: Atlantic Ocean, coast of South America (modern Venezuela, Colombia)
events.description: Amerigo Vespucci, a Florentine merchant and navigator, embarked on his second voyage to the Americas in 1499, sailing under the Spanish flag. This expedition explored the coast of South America, particularly the region of modern-day Venezuela and Colombia. Vespucci's detailed accounts of the voyage, published in letters and later compilations, provided Europeans with vivid descriptions of the lands, peoples, and resources of the New World. His observations contributed to the growing recognition that the lands discovered by Columbus were a previously unknown continent, not part of Asia.
key_figures: Alonso de Ojeda (co-commander of the expedition)
Juan de la Cosa (cartographer and navigator)

sources.name: Vespucci's letters, particularly accounts of his voyages
Spanish royal records and navigational charts
Later compilations such as the Paesi Novamente Retrovati (Newly Discovered Lands)

events.certainty_note: The dating of Vespucci's voyages is notoriously uncertain. Scholars debate the exact dates and sequence of his voyages. The 1499 date for his second voyage is widely accepted but not universally agreed upon. Vespucci's own accounts are sometimes contradictory, and later compilations may have altered details.

events.title: Savonarola's execution in Florence; end of the Florentine theocracy
events.description: Girolamo Savonarola, a Dominican friar and religious reformer who had wielded significant political influence in Florence, was executed by hanging and burning on May 23, 1498. However, the aftermath of his execution continued to shape Florentine politics in 1499. Savonarola had led a theocratic government in Florence from 1494 to 1498, promoting religious reform and moral renewal. His execution marked the end of his direct influence, but his legacy continued to influence Florentine religious and political thought. The year 1499 saw the consolidation of a more secular, humanist-oriented government in Florence, reflecting the broader trends of the Italian Renaissance.
key_figures: Piero Soderini (Gonfaloniere of Florence, r. 1502–1512, not yet in power in 1499)
Lorenzo de' Medici (de facto ruler of Florence, died 1492)

sources.name: Florentine state records and chronicles
Contemporary accounts by Florentine observers and visitors
Later Florentine historiography

events.certainty_note: Savonarola's execution occurred in 1498, not 1499. However, the political aftermath and consolidation of secular rule continued into 1499. The year 1499 is included here to mark the transition from theocratic to secular governance in Florence.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure voyages continue; Ming Dynasty maritime expeditions
events.region: Indian Ocean, Southeast Asia, East Africa
events.description: In 1499, the Ming Dynasty of China was in the midst of its great maritime expeditions under the command of the eunuch admiral Zheng He. Zheng He's treasure voyages (1405–1433) were among the largest and most sophisticated maritime expeditions of the pre-modern world, involving massive treasure ships and fleets that explored the Indian Ocean, visited East Africa, and established Chinese diplomatic and trade networks. By 1499, these voyages were in their later stages, but they represented the height of Chinese maritime power and technological achievement. The voyages demonstrated Chinese naval superiority and the sophistication of Ming Dynasty navigation and shipbuilding.
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (r. 1402–1424, patron of earlier voyages)
Hongwu Emperor's successors (patrons of later voyages)

sources.name: Ming Dynasty court records and the Mingshi (History of the Ming Dynasty)
The Yingya Shenglan (Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores) by Ma Huan, a participant in Zheng He's voyages
Archaeological evidence from shipwrecks and coastal sites

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's voyages are well-documented in Chinese sources. However, the exact dates and details of individual voyages are sometimes uncertain. By 1499, the voyages had ended (around 1433), though their legacy remained.

disconfirming_evidence: Vasco da Gama's voyage is sometimes portrayed as the singular moment of European maritime triumph, but it was built on decades of Portuguese exploration and technological development, particularly the work of Prince Henry the Navigator and earlier explorers like Bartolomeu Dias. Additionally, da Gama's treatment of Indian merchants and rulers was often hostile and exploitative, a fact sometimes glossed over in triumphalist accounts. The First Venetian-Ottoman War is sometimes presented as a simple conflict between Christian and Muslim powers, but it was fundamentally a struggle for control of trade routes and territorial dominance, with religious identity being secondary to economic and political interests. Savonarola's execution occurred in 1498, not 1499, though the political aftermath continued into 1499; the inclusion of this event in 1499 is somewhat arbitrary and reflects the ongoing influence of his legacy rather than a specific event in 1499 itself.
historiographic_note: The year 1499 is well-documented for European history, particularly Portuguese maritime exploration and Mediterranean politics, which are extensively recorded in royal archives and contemporary accounts. However, the documentation is heavily weighted toward European perspectives and interests. For the Americas, documentation is sparse and comes primarily from Spanish and Portuguese sources written by explorers; indigenous perspectives are largely absent. For Asia, Chinese sources are abundant but are underrepresented in Western scholarship; the Ming Dynasty's maritime activities are well-documented in Chinese archives but less familiar to Western historians. African and Southeast Asian perspectives are largely absent from surviving written records accessible to modern scholarship. The year 1499 is often treated as a pivotal moment in world history (the return of Vasco da Gama, the confirmation of the sea route to India), but this periodization reflects a Eurocentric bias and obscures the continued vitality and sophistication of non-European civilizations at this time.

The 1499 beginning of the First Venetian-Ottoman War continued through 1501 and beyond.
Vespucci's 1499 voyage led to his third voyage in 1501.

The Aztec and Inca empires are at their territorial peaks.

events.title: Vasco da Gama reaches India; arrival in Calicut
events.region: Indian Ocean; Calicut (modern Kozhikode, Kerala, India)
events.description: Vasco da Gama, commanding a Portuguese fleet of four ships, reached Calicut (modern Kozhikode) on the southwestern coast of India on May 20, 1498. This was the first documented European sea voyage to India, completing a journey that had begun in Portugal in July 1497. Da Gama's arrival in India confirmed the viability of the sea route around the Cape of Good Hope and demonstrated Portuguese maritime superiority. The voyage was a triumph of Portuguese navigation, shipbuilding, and determination. Da Gama's arrival in India initiated a new era of European-Asian contact and trade, fundamentally altering global commerce and geopolitics. The voyage also demonstrated the feasibility of European maritime expansion into Asian waters.

sources.name: Portuguese royal chronicles and letters
Roteiros (pilot books) and navigational records
Later Portuguese historiography, particularly by João de Barros

events.certainty_note: Da Gama's arrival in Calicut on May 20, 1498, is exceptionally well-documented in Portuguese sources. The date is secure.

events.title: Savonarola's execution in Florence
events.description: Girolamo Savonarola, a Dominican friar and religious reformer who had wielded significant political influence in Florence, was executed by hanging and burning on May 23, 1498. Savonarola had led a theocratic government in Florence from 1494 to 1498, promoting religious reform and moral renewal. His government had been marked by the burning of 'vanities' (books, art, and other items deemed sinful) and the expulsion of the Medici family from Florence. However, his influence waned as his prophecies failed to materialize and his moral authority was questioned. He was arrested, tried for heresy, and executed. His death marked the end of the theocratic experiment in Florence and the beginning of a more secular, humanist-oriented government.

sources.name: Florentine state records and chronicles
Contemporary accounts by Florentine observers and visitors
Later Florentine historiography

events.certainty_note: Savonarola's execution on May 23, 1498, is well-documented in Florentine sources. The date is secure.

events.title: Columbus's third voyage to the Americas; discovery of Trinidad and the South American mainland
events.region: Atlantic Ocean and Caribbean; Trinidad and the coast of South America (modern Venezuela)
events.description: Christopher Columbus, commanding a fleet of six ships, embarked on his third voyage to the Americas in May 1498. This expedition explored new territories, including the island of Trinidad and the coast of South America (modern Venezuela). Columbus's arrival on the South American mainland was significant, as it represented the first documented European contact with the continental landmass of the Americas. Columbus believed he had reached the coast of a large continent, possibly the location of the Earthly Paradise described in medieval cosmography. The voyage was marked by difficulties, including storms, disease, and conflicts with indigenous peoples and Spanish colonists. Columbus was eventually arrested and returned to Spain in chains, accused of mismanagement of the colony.
key_figures: King Ferdinand and Queen Isabella of Spain (patrons)
Indigenous peoples of Trinidad and Venezuela

sources.name: Columbus's letters and journals
Spanish royal records and chronicles
Later Spanish historiography, particularly by Bartolomé de las Casas

events.certainty_note: Columbus's third voyage is well-documented in Spanish sources and Columbus's own letters. The dates (May 1498 departure, arrival in Trinidad in July 1498) are secure.

Zheng He's treasure voyages (1405–1433) were among the largest and most sophisticated maritime expeditions of the pre-modern world, involving massive treasure ships and fleets that explored the Indian Ocean, visited East Africa, and established Chinese diplomatic and trade networks. By 1498, these voyages were in their later stages, but they represented the height of Chinese maritime power and technological achievement. The voyages demonstrated Chinese naval superiority and the sophistication of Ming Dynasty navigation and shipbuilding.

events.title: Ahuitzotl rules the Aztec Empire; expansion and consolidation
events.description: In 1498, Ahuitzotl (r. 1486–1502) was the eighth emperor of the Aztec Empire, ruling during a period of territorial expansion and consolidation. Ahuitzotl had expanded Aztec territory through military campaigns and had consolidated imperial power through administrative reforms. He was known for his military prowess and his patronage of architecture and art. In 1498, Ahuitzotl was in the latter part of his reign, and the Aztec Empire was at the height of its territorial extent and power. Ahuitzotl would die in 1502, to be succeeded by his nephew Montezuma II.

sources.name: Aztec codices, particularly the Codex Mendoza and the Florentine Codex
Spanish chronicles by Bernal Díaz del Castillo and others
Nahuatl-language sources compiled after the Spanish conquest

events.certainty_note: Ahuitzotl's reign (1486–1502) is well-documented in Aztec codices and Spanish chronicles. The year 1498 falls within his reign, and the general political situation is well-attested.
cross_references: 1502-001 (Death of Ahuitzotl; succession of Montezuma II)

disconfirming_evidence: Vasco da Gama's voyage is sometimes portrayed as the singular moment of European maritime triumph, but it was built on decades of Portuguese exploration and technological development, particularly the work of Prince Henry the Navigator and earlier explorers like Bartolomeu Dias. Additionally, da Gama's treatment of Indian merchants and rulers was often hostile and exploitative, a fact sometimes glossed over in triumphalist accounts. Columbus's third voyage is sometimes presented as a triumphant discovery, but it was marked by difficulties, disease, and conflict. Columbus's belief that he had reached the Earthly Paradise was based on medieval cosmography rather than accurate geographical understanding. Savonarola's execution is sometimes portrayed as a simple conflict between religious reform and papal authority, but it was fundamentally a political struggle involving the Medici family, the Florentine oligarchy, and competing visions of Florentine governance.
historiographic_note: The year 1498 is well-documented for European history, particularly Portuguese and Spanish maritime exploration, which are extensively recorded in royal archives and contemporary accounts. However, the documentation is heavily weighted toward European perspectives and interests. For the Americas, documentation is sparse and comes primarily from Spanish sources written by Columbus and later chroniclers; indigenous perspectives are largely absent. For Asia, Chinese sources are abundant but are underrepresented in Western scholarship; the Ming Dynasty's maritime activities are well-documented in Chinese archives but less familiar to Western historians. African and Southeast Asian perspectives are largely absent from surviving written records accessible to modern scholarship. The year 1498 is often treated as a pivotal moment in world history (da Gama's arrival in India, Columbus's third voyage), but this periodization reflects a Eurocentric bias and obscures the continued vitality and sophistication of non-European civilizations at this time.

events.title: Vasco da Gama departs from Portugal on his voyage to India
events.region: Atlantic Ocean; Portugal to Cape of Good Hope
events.description: Vasco da Gama, commanding a fleet of four ships (the São Gabriel, São Rafael, Berrio, and a supply ship), departed from Lisbon on July 8, 1497, on a voyage intended to reach India by sailing around the Cape of Good Hope. This was the first European sea voyage explicitly intended to reach India via the Cape route. Da Gama's expedition was the culmination of decades of Portuguese maritime exploration and technological development. The voyage would take nearly two years, with da Gama reaching Calicut in May 1498 and returning to Portugal in September 1499. The voyage was a triumph of Portuguese navigation, shipbuilding, and determination, and it fundamentally altered European understanding of global geography and trade routes.
key_figures: Prince Henry the Navigator (deceased 1460, but his legacy of exploration continued)

sources.name: Portuguese royal chronicles and letters
Roteiros (pilot books) and navigational records
Later Portuguese historiography, particularly by João de Barros

events.certainty_note: Da Gama's departure from Lisbon on July 8, 1497, is exceptionally well-documented in Portuguese sources. The date is secure.

events.title: John Cabot's voyage to North America; exploration of the Atlantic coast
events.region: Atlantic Ocean; coast of North America (modern Newfoundland and Nova Scotia)
events.description: John Cabot (Giovanni Caboto), a Venetian navigator sailing under the English flag, departed from Bristol in May 1497 on a voyage to explore the Atlantic coast of North America. Cabot's expedition reached the coast of North America (likely modern Newfoundland or Nova Scotia) in June 1497. Cabot's voyage was significant as the first documented European exploration of the North American coast since the Norse voyages of the 10th–11th centuries. Cabot's voyage established English claims to North America and demonstrated the feasibility of transatlantic voyages from northern European ports. The voyage was sponsored by King Henry VII of England and was intended to find a westward route to Asia.

sources.name: English royal records and letters patent
Contemporary accounts by English observers and merchants
Later English historiography

events.certainty_note: The dating of Cabot's voyage is secure (May–June 1497), but the exact location of his landfall is uncertain. Scholars debate whether he reached Newfoundland, Nova Scotia, or another location on the North American coast. The voyage is well-attested in English royal records.

Ahuitzotl had expanded Aztec territory through military campaigns and had consolidated imperial power through administrative reforms. He was known for his military prowess and his patronage of architecture and art. In 1497, Ahuitzotl was in the middle of his reign, and the Aztec Empire was expanding its territorial control and consolidating its power. Ahuitzotl would continue to rule until his death in 1502.
