era_context: The mid-15th century marks the height of the European Renaissance in Italy, the consolidation of the Ming Dynasty in China (r. 1368–1644), the expansion of the Ottoman Empire in the Eastern Mediterranean and Balkans, and the early phase of Portuguese maritime exploration along the African coast. In Mesoamerica, the Aztec Empire under Montezuma I was expanding its tributary network. This is an era of technological innovation (printing press, improved navigation), political centralisation, and increasing global interconnection through trade and exploration.

events.title: Portuguese slave raid on Cape Bojador; first documented European enslavement of West African peoples
events.region: West Africa (Cape Bojador, present-day Western Sahara)
events.description: Portuguese explorers under the command of Antão Gonçalves and Nuno Tristão captured approximately 12 Africans (Berbers and sub-Saharan peoples) near Cape Bojador, marking the beginning of the transatlantic slave trade. This raid, sanctioned by Prince Henry the Navigator, initiated a systematic European trade in enslaved Africans that would expand dramatically over the following centuries. The captives were brought to Lagos, Portugal, and subsequently sold. This event is foundational to the history of the Atlantic slave trade and European colonialism.
key_figures: Prince Henry the Navigator (Portuguese royal patron)

sources.name: Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Crónica dos Feitos da Guiné (Chronicle of the Discovery of Guinea), written c. 1453–1460
Portuguese royal records and administrative documents

events.certainty_note: The event is well-attested in Portuguese chronicles and administrative records. Zurara's account, though written ~12 years later, is based on contemporary documentation and eyewitness testimony. The date 1441 is accepted by modern scholarship.
cross_references: Follows from 1434-001 (Portuguese exploration of African coast)
Led to 1444-001 (first large-scale slave auction in Lagos)

events.title: Zheng He's treasure fleet returns to China; end of major Ming maritime expeditions
events.description: Admiral Zheng He's seventh and final treasure fleet expedition returned to China in 1441 after departing in 1433. This voyage, like its predecessors, was a massive demonstration of Ming naval power and diplomatic reach, visiting East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and Southeast Asia. However, this expedition marked the effective end of China's great maritime voyages. Following Zheng He's death in 1433 and increasing pressure from Confucian officials who viewed oceanic exploration as wasteful and contrary to Confucian values, the Ming court gradually abandoned support for such expeditions. The treasure ships were subsequently dismantled or left to rot, and maritime activity was restricted. This represents a pivotal moment in global history: China's withdrawal from oceanic exploration left the field open to European powers.
key_figures: Zheng He (Admiral, though deceased by 1441; his legacy)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty), official court records
Ma Huan, Yingya Shenglan (The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores), written c. 1433–1451
Historiographic consensus in modern sinology

events.certainty_note: The return date of 1441 is established in Ming court records. The broader context of the end of the treasure voyages is well-documented, though the precise decision-making process involved multiple factors over several years.
cross_references: Led to 1500s-onwards (European dominance of oceanic trade)

events.title: Gutenberg's printing press development; early experiments with movable type in Europe
events.description: Johannes Gutenberg was actively developing his movable-type printing press in Mainz during the early 1440s. While the precise date of the press's invention is debated (estimates range from 1440–1450), 1441 falls within the period of intensive experimentation and refinement. Gutenberg's innovation combined existing technologies—the screw press (used in wine-making), movable metal type (known in East Asia for centuries), and oil-based inks—into a practical system for mass-producing books in Europe. This technology would revolutionise the dissemination of knowledge, support the Protestant Reformation, and accelerate the Scientific Revolution. The printing press is considered one of the most transformative inventions in human history.

sources.name: Indirect evidence from Gutenberg's later legal disputes and business records (1450s–1460s)
Historiographic consensus based on technical analysis and documentary evidence

events.certainty_note: The exact year of the printing press's invention is uncertain. 1441 is assigned by convention within the period of active development. The first documented printed books (the Gutenberg Bible) date to c. 1455. Earlier dates (1440) and later dates (1450) are also proposed by scholars.
cross_references: Part of broader technological revolution of the 15th century

events.title: Inca Empire under Pachacuti continues expansion; conquest of the Chincha Kingdom
events.description: Under the leadership of Pachacuti Inca Yupanqui (r. 1438–1471), the Inca Empire continued its rapid territorial expansion. Around this period (dating is approximate), the Inca conquered the Chincha Kingdom, a wealthy coastal polity in present-day southern Peru. The Chincha were known for their maritime trade networks and agricultural wealth. Their incorporation into the Inca Empire expanded Inca control over coastal resources and trade routes. Pachacuti's reign saw the transformation of the Inca state from a regional power into a vast empire spanning much of western South America. This conquest was part of a broader campaign of military expansion and administrative reorganisation.

sources.name: Quipucamayoc (Inca record-keepers) oral traditions, recorded by Spanish chroniclers in the 16th century
Pedro Cieza de León, Crónica del Perú (Chronicle of Peru), written 1550s
Archaeological evidence from Inca administrative sites

events.certainty_note: The Inca did not use a written calendar system comparable to the Gregorian calendar. Dates for Inca events are reconstructed from oral traditions recorded by Spanish chroniclers decades or centuries after the fact. The conquest of Chincha is traditionally dated to Pachacuti's reign (1438–1471), but the precise year is uncertain. 1441 is assigned by convention within this period.

events.title: Ottoman siege and conquest of Varna; Ottoman expansion into the Balkans
events.description: The Ottoman Empire, under Sultan Murad II, besieged and conquered the fortress city of Varna on the Black Sea coast in 1441. Varna had been held by Venice and Hungary as a strategic stronghold against Ottoman expansion. The Ottoman conquest of Varna consolidated Ottoman control over the Bulgarian coast and the western Black Sea, eliminating a major obstacle to Ottoman dominance in the Balkans. This victory was part of a broader pattern of Ottoman expansion into southeastern Europe during the 15th century. The fall of Varna demonstrated Ottoman military superiority and signalled the decline of Christian resistance in the region.

sources.name: Ottoman court records and chronicles (Takvim-i Vekayi)
Venetian and Hungarian diplomatic records
Historiographic consensus in Ottoman and Balkan studies

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the Portuguese slave raid at Cape Bojador (1441-001) is sometimes overstated in popular histories as the 'beginning' of the transatlantic slave trade. However, slavery and slave trading were already well-established in the Mediterranean and Islamic world. The Portuguese raid represents the beginning of European participation in the African slave trade and the eventual transatlantic system, not the invention of slavery itself. Additionally, some scholars argue that the scale of early Portuguese slaving (1441–1460s) was modest compared to later centuries and should not be anachronistically projected backward. The dating of Gutenberg's press (1441-003) is highly uncertain; some scholars prefer 1440 or 1450. No contemporary documentation of the press's invention survives from 1441 itself.
historiographic_note: The year 1441 is well-documented for European, Ottoman, and East Asian events, reflecting the bias of surviving sources toward literate, centralised states with written record-keeping. The Portuguese slave raid is known primarily through later Portuguese chronicles (Zurara, written c. 1453–1460), which may have been shaped by royal propaganda. Inca history for this period relies entirely on oral traditions recorded by Spanish chroniclers in the 16th century, introducing significant uncertainty about precise dating and interpretation. African, indigenous American, and many Asian societies' perspectives on these events are largely absent from surviving sources, reflecting the colonial and Eurocentric bias of the historical record.

graph_edges.note: The 1441 raid initiated Portuguese slaving; the 1444 Lagos auction was the first large-scale sale of enslaved Africans in Europe.
China's withdrawal from oceanic exploration left the Atlantic and Indian Oceans open to European expansion.
Gutenberg's press development culminated in the printing of the Gutenberg Bible c. 1455.
The Chincha conquest was part of Pachacuti's broader expansion campaign (1438–1471).
Ottoman expansion in the Balkans, including the conquest of Varna, contributed to the Ottoman siege of Constantinople in 1453.

era_context: The mid-15th century is marked by the consolidation of centralised nation-states in Europe (England, France, Spain, Portugal), the height of the Italian Renaissance, the Ming Dynasty's cultural and administrative peak in China, Ottoman expansion in the Eastern Mediterranean, and the Inca Empire's rapid territorial growth in South America. This era is characterised by technological innovation, increased maritime exploration, and the beginning of European global expansion.

events.title: Eton College founded by King Henry VI of England
events.description: King Henry VI of England founded Eton College, a prestigious school in Eton, Berkshire, as part of his broader educational and religious patronage. The college was established as a feeder school for King's College, Cambridge, which Henry had also founded in 1441. Eton College became one of the most influential educational institutions in England and the English-speaking world, educating generations of political, military, and intellectual leaders. The founding reflects the 15th-century trend among European monarchs of patronising education and learning as a means of consolidating royal authority and promoting humanist values.

sources.name: Royal charter and founding documents of Eton College
English royal records (Patent Rolls)

events.certainty_note: The founding of Eton College in 1440 is well-documented in royal charters and administrative records. The date is secure.

events.title: Pachacuti Inca Yupanqui becomes Inca ruler; beginning of rapid Inca expansion
events.description: Pachacuti Inca Yupanqui ascended to power as the ninth Inca ruler, either in 1438 or 1440 (sources vary). His accession marked the beginning of a dramatic transformation of the Inca state from a regional power into a vast empire. Pachacuti initiated a series of military campaigns that expanded Inca territory from the Cusco region to encompass much of the Andes, including present-day Peru, Bolivia, Ecuador, and parts of Chile and Argentina. He also reorganised the Inca administrative system, introducing the mit'a labour system and the quipu record-keeping system on a larger scale. Pachacuti's reign (traditionally dated 1438–1471) is considered the foundation of the Inca Empire as a major imperial power.

sources.name: Quipucamayoc oral traditions, recorded by Spanish chroniclers in the 16th century
Pedro Cieza de León, Crónica del Perú (Chronicle of Peru), written 1550s
Garcilaso de la Vega, Royal Commentaries of the Incas, written early 17th century

events.certainty_note: The Inca did not use a written calendar system. Dates for Inca events are reconstructed from oral traditions recorded by Spanish chroniclers decades or centuries later. Pachacuti's accession is traditionally dated to either 1438 or 1440; modern scholarship remains divided. The date 1440 is assigned here as an alternative to 1438.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure fleet departs on seventh voyage; final major Ming maritime expedition
events.description: Admiral Zheng He's seventh and final treasure fleet expedition departed from China in 1433 and was still active in 1440, visiting ports in East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and Southeast Asia. This massive fleet, comprising hundreds of ships and tens of thousands of personnel, was a demonstration of Ming naval power and diplomatic reach. The expedition carried gifts, traded goods, and conducted diplomatic missions on behalf of the Ming court. However, this voyage would be the last of its kind. Following Zheng He's death in 1433 and increasing opposition from Confucian officials who viewed oceanic exploration as wasteful, the Ming court would gradually abandon support for such expeditions after 1441.
key_figures: Zheng He (Admiral, though deceased by 1440; his legacy)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty), official court records
Ma Huan, Yingya Shenglan (The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores), written c. 1433–1451

events.certainty_note: The seventh voyage's timeline (1433–1441) is established in Ming court records. The year 1440 falls within the documented period of this expedition.
cross_references: Led to 1441-002 (return of the fleet and end of major expeditions)

events.title: Gutenberg's printing press; continued development of movable-type technology
events.description: Johannes Gutenberg was actively developing his movable-type printing press in Mainz during 1440. This year falls within the period of intensive experimentation and refinement of the technology. Gutenberg's innovation combined the screw press (used in wine-making), movable metal type (known in East Asia for centuries), and oil-based inks into a practical system for mass-producing books in Europe. The exact date of the press's invention is debated among scholars, with estimates ranging from 1440 to 1450. The first documented printed books (the Gutenberg Bible) date to c. 1455.

sources.name: Indirect evidence from Gutenberg's later legal disputes and business records (1450s–1460s)
Historiographic consensus based on technical analysis

events.certainty_note: The exact year of the printing press's invention is uncertain. 1440 is one of several proposed dates. Some scholars prefer 1441 or 1450. No contemporary documentation of the press's invention from 1440 survives.

events.title: Ottoman Empire consolidates control over the Balkans; Murad II's campaigns
events.description: Under Sultan Murad II (r. 1421–1444, 1446–1451), the Ottoman Empire continued its systematic conquest and consolidation of the Balkans. In 1440, Ottoman forces were engaged in campaigns against Hungarian and Venetian strongholds in the region. The Ottomans were gradually eliminating Christian resistance and establishing Ottoman administrative control over former Byzantine and Serbian territories. This period saw the transformation of the Ottoman state from a regional Anatolian power into a major Balkan and Mediterranean power. The conquest of key fortresses and the subjugation of local rulers were part of a broader strategy of territorial expansion and administrative centralisation.

sources.name: Ottoman court records and chronicles
Venetian and Hungarian diplomatic records

events.certainty_note: Ottoman military campaigns in the Balkans during 1440 are well-documented in Ottoman, Venetian, and Hungarian sources.
cross_references: Part of broader Ottoman expansion in the 15th century

disconfirming_evidence: The founding of Eton College (1440-001) is sometimes presented as a unique or exceptional act of royal patronage, but educational patronage was common among 15th-century European monarchs. The significance of Eton lies not in its novelty but in its long-term institutional influence. Regarding Pachacuti's accession (1440-002), some sources date it to 1438, others to 1440, and a few to 1442. The uncertainty reflects the absence of written Inca records and the reliance on oral traditions recorded by Spanish chroniclers. The dating of Gutenberg's press (1440-004) remains contested; some scholars argue for 1441 or 1450 instead of 1440.
historiographic_note: The year 1440 is well-documented for European, Ottoman, and East Asian events, reflecting the bias of surviving sources toward literate, centralised states. Inca history relies entirely on oral traditions recorded by Spanish chroniclers in the 16th century, introducing significant uncertainty. African, indigenous American, and many Asian societies' perspectives on these events are largely absent from surviving sources. The historical record for 1440 is shaped by the priorities and biases of European, Ottoman, and Chinese chroniclers.

era_context: The mid-15th century is a period of significant political, technological, and cultural transformation across multiple civilisations. In Europe, the Renaissance is flourishing in Italy, and centralised nation-states are consolidating power. The Ming Dynasty in China is at its cultural and administrative peak. The Ottoman Empire is expanding into the Balkans. In Mesoamerica, the Aztec Empire is expanding under Montezuma I. This era is marked by technological innovation, increased maritime exploration, and the beginning of European global expansion.

events.title: Council of Florence; union of Eastern and Western Christian churches (temporary)
events.description: The Council of Florence (1438–1445) was a major ecumenical council of the Catholic Church that attempted to reunite the Eastern Orthodox Church and the Roman Catholic Church, which had been in schism since 1054. In 1439, the council reached a temporary agreement on the union of the churches, known as the Decree of Union. The agreement was signed by Pope Eugenius IV and representatives of the Eastern Orthodox Church, including the Patriarch of Constantinople. However, the union was short-lived; it was rejected by many Orthodox Christians and formally repudiated after the Ottoman conquest of Constantinople in 1453. The council is significant as an attempt at Christian reconciliation and as a reflection of the broader intellectual and diplomatic currents of the Renaissance.
key_figures: Pope Eugenius IV (Roman Catholic Pope, r. 1431–1447)
Patriarch Joseph II of Constantinople (Eastern Orthodox Patriarch)
Emperor John VIII Palaiologus (Byzantine Emperor, r. 1425–1448)

events.title: Printing press development; Gutenberg's continued experimentation in Mainz

Gutenberg's innovation combined the screw press (used in wine-making), movable metal type (known in East Asia for centuries), and oil-based inks into a practical system for mass-producing books in Europe. The exact date of the press's invention is debated among scholars, with estimates ranging from 1440 to 1450. The first documented printed books (the Gutenberg Bible) date to c. 1455.

events.title: Portuguese maritime exploration; continued expansion along the African coast
events.description: Portuguese explorers, sponsored by Prince Henry the Navigator, continued their systematic exploration of the West African coast in 1439. Portuguese ships were gradually pushing further south along the coast, establishing trading posts and gathering information about African geography, peoples, and resources. This period saw the beginning of Portuguese commercial interest in African gold, ivory, and other goods. The exploration was motivated by a combination of factors: the desire for wealth, the spread of Christianity, and the accumulation of geographical knowledge. These voyages laid the groundwork for the later Portuguese slave trade and European colonialism in Africa.
key_figures: Prince Henry the Navigator (Portuguese royal patron)

events.title: Ming Dynasty; Emperor Yingzong's reign begins; cultural and administrative consolidation
events.description: Emperor Yingzong ascended to the Ming throne in 1435 and continued his reign in 1439. His reign (r. 1435–1449, 1457–1464) was marked by cultural and administrative consolidation. The Ming Dynasty was at its height during this period, with a strong centralised government, a flourishing economy, and significant cultural achievements in art, literature, and architecture. However, Yingzong's reign also saw the gradual withdrawal of support for maritime exploration, as Confucian officials increasingly opposed the treasure voyages as wasteful. The Ming court was focused on internal consolidation and defence against Mongol incursions from the north.

events.title: Zheng He's final voyage returns to China
events.description: Admiral Zheng He's seventh and final treasure voyage fleet returns to Nanjing after departing in 1433. events.certainty_note: Voyage dates and return are well-documented in Ming court records and contemporary accounts by voyage participants.

events.title: Gutenberg begins printing experiments with movable type
events.description: Johannes Gutenberg, a goldsmith and printer in Mainz, is believed to have begun systematic experimentation with movable metal type printing around this period (exact dating disputed; 1436–1440 is the scholarly consensus window). His innovation combined existing technologies—the screw press, metal casting, and oil-based inks—into a practical system for mass-producing books. This development would revolutionize information distribution across Europe, though the Gutenberg Bible itself was not completed until c. 1455.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus (no contemporary documentation of Gutenberg's early work survives)
Later legal records and guild documents mentioning Gutenberg (16th–17th century)

events.certainty_note: No contemporary records document Gutenberg's experiments. Dating relies on later biographical accounts, legal disputes, and reverse-engineering from the Gutenberg Bible's completion date. The window 1436–1440 is conventional; the exact year is not confirmed.

events.title: Council of Florence convenes on church union
events.description: The Council of Florence (1434–1443) continues its work toward reconciling the Eastern Orthodox Church with the Roman Catholic Church. In 1436, delegates from Constantinople, including Patriarch Joseph II, are present to negotiate union. The council represents a major diplomatic effort by Pope Eugenius IV to heal the Great Schism of 1054, driven partly by Ottoman pressure on Byzantine territories. Though the union agreed upon would prove short-lived, the council's proceedings shaped Renaissance intellectual life through the presence of Greek scholars and texts.

sources.name: Council of Florence official acts and decrees
Contemporary chronicles by attendees (e.g., Vespasiano da Bisticci)

events.certainty_note: Council proceedings are well-documented in official records and contemporary accounts.

events.title: Joan of Arc canonization process initiated (traditional dating)
events.description: Following Joan of Arc's execution in 1431, her mother Isabelle Romée petitioned Pope Callixtus III for a retrial and rehabilitation of her daughter's memory. The formal nullification trial (Procès en Nullité) began in 1456, but preliminary ecclesiastical inquiries and advocacy efforts occurred in the mid-1430s. Joan's case became a symbol of French national identity and Catholic martyrdom, though her canonization would not occur until 1920.

sources.name: Procès en Nullité records (1456 onward, documenting earlier petitions)
Later hagiographic accounts

events.certainty_note: The exact timing of initial rehabilitation efforts is unclear; 1436 is a conventional date within the broader 1430s–1450s period of advocacy.

events.title: Inca Empire consolidates Cusco valley under Pachacuti (traditional dating)
events.description: Pachacuti Inca Yupanqui, the ninth Inca ruler, is traditionally dated to have begun his reign around 1438–1471, though some sources place his rise earlier. By the mid-1430s, he was consolidating power in the Cusco valley and initiating the military campaigns that would expand the Inca Empire from a regional power into a vast multi-ethnic state. His reign marked a transition from the earlier Inca confederation to a centralized imperial system with sophisticated administrative, military, and religious structures.

sources.name: Quipucamayoc (Inca record-keepers' oral traditions), recorded by Spanish chroniclers (16th century)
Spanish colonial chronicles (Cieza de León, Garcilaso de la Vega)

events.certainty_note: Inca chronology relies entirely on oral traditions recorded by Spanish chroniclers a century after events. No contemporary written records exist. Dating is approximate and debated; some scholars place Pachacuti's reign 50–100 years earlier or later.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Gutenberg's printing experiments (1436-002) is highly uncertain; some scholars argue for dates as late as 1450, and the claim that he began in 1436 rests on no contemporary evidence. The Inca chronology (1436-005) is contested: some scholars argue Pachacuti's reign should be dated 50–100 years earlier based on archaeological and astronomical evidence, making the traditional Spanish-chronicled dates unreliable. Joan of Arc's rehabilitation (1436-004) is loosely dated; formal proceedings began in 1456, and the 1436 date is speculative.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1436 is uneven. European events (printing, church councils, Joan of Arc's aftermath) are moderately well-attested in chronicles and official records, though dating uncertainties persist. Chinese sources are rich for Zheng He's voyages. Inca history is entirely dependent on oral traditions filtered through Spanish colonial narratives, introducing significant bias and chronological uncertainty. Sub-Saharan African, Southeast Asian, and Mesoamerican events are underrepresented in surviving records accessible to Western scholarship.

events.title: Death of Xuande Emperor; succession crisis in Ming China
events.description: The Xuande Emperor (r. 1426–1435) dies in January 1435 at age 38, ending a reign marked by cultural flourishing, military consolidation, and the continuation of Zheng He's treasure voyages. He is succeeded by his eight-year-old son, the Zhengtong Emperor, whose reign is dominated by regency politics and the influence of the eunuch Wang Zhen. This succession marks a turning point: the new emperor's youth and the eunuch faction's ascendancy will lead to the cessation of maritime expeditions and a shift toward isolationism and land-based defense.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Official Ming Records)
Ming court chronicles

events.certainty_note: The Xuande Emperor's death date and succession are precisely documented in Ming court records.
cross_references: 1449-001 (Tumu Crisis, consequence of Wang Zhen's influence)

events.title: Treaty of Arras between Burgundy and France
events.description: The Treaty of Arras (September 1435) marks a major diplomatic realignment in the Hundred Years' War. Duke Philip the Good of Burgundy, a long-time English ally, reconciles with King Charles VII of France, abandoning his English alliance. This treaty effectively isolates England, accelerating the end of English dominance in France and contributing to the final phase of the Hundred Years' War. The treaty also reflects the rise of Burgundy as an independent power broker in European politics.

sources.name: Treaty of Arras (official text)
Contemporary chronicles (Monstrelet, Chastellain)

events.certainty_note: The treaty is well-documented in official records and contemporary chronicles.

events.title: Council of Florence continues; Greek scholars arrive
events.description: The Council of Florence (1434–1443) continues its work toward church union. In 1435, Greek Orthodox delegates and scholars, including the philosopher Gemistus Pletho, arrive in Florence. Their presence catalyzes the transmission of Greek classical texts and philosophical ideas to the Italian Renaissance, influencing humanist scholars like Cosimo de' Medici and Marsilio Ficino. This intellectual exchange, though motivated by ecclesiastical politics, becomes a major vector for the revival of classical learning.
key_figures: Cosimo de' Medici (Florence, patron of learning)

sources.name: Council of Florence records
Vespasiano da Bisticci, Lives of Illustrious Men

events.certainty_note: The arrival of Greek scholars at the council is well-documented.

events.title: Portuguese exploration of West African coast continues
events.description: Under the patronage of Prince Henry the Navigator, Portuguese caravels continue systematic exploration of the West African coast. By 1435, Portuguese traders have established contacts with Berber and sub-Saharan African merchants, initiating the Atlantic slave trade (initially small-scale) and seeking gold, spices, and geographic knowledge. These voyages, though modest compared to later expeditions, represent the beginning of European maritime expansion and the eventual circumnavigation of Africa.

sources.name: Portuguese royal chronicles and navigational records
Later accounts by Gomes Eanes de Zurara (Crónica da Guiné)

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration in 1435 is attested in later chronicles; contemporary documentation is sparse.
cross_references: 1444-001 (First large-scale slave raid from West Africa)
1488-001 (Bartolomeu Dias rounds Cape of Good Hope)

events.title: Hussite Wars conclude in Bohemia
events.description: The Hussite Wars (1419–1434), a series of religious and political conflicts in Bohemia following Jan Hus's execution in 1415, effectively conclude by 1435. The radical Taborite faction is defeated, and the moderate Utraquist church gains recognition within the Holy Roman Empire. The wars represent one of the earliest major religious conflicts in Christian Europe and establish Bohemia as a center of religious reform, prefiguring the later Protestant Reformation.
key_figures: Sigismund (Holy Roman Emperor, King of Bohemia)

sources.name: Bohemian chronicles and church records
Holy Roman Empire official records

events.certainty_note: The end of the Hussite Wars is well-documented in contemporary records.

disconfirming_evidence: The Treaty of Arras (1435-002) is sometimes portrayed as the decisive blow to English power in France, but England retained Calais and other territories until 1558, and English claims to French territory persisted longer. Portuguese exploration (1435-004) is often romanticized as a unified program under Henry the Navigator; in reality, early voyages were sporadic and motivated by diverse commercial and political interests. The Hussite Wars' conclusion (1435-005) is dated to 1434–1435, but some sources extend conflict into the 1440s, and the religious settlement remained contested.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1435 is moderate. European political and military events are well-attested in chronicles and official records. Chinese court records are rich for the succession. Portuguese exploration is documented in later chronicles but contemporary records are sparse. African perspectives on Portuguese contact are almost entirely absent from surviving sources, reflecting the colonial bias of European documentation.

events.title: Council of Florence convenes
events.description: Pope Eugenius IV convenes the Council of Florence (1434–1443) to address church reform and the reconciliation of the Eastern Orthodox Church with Rome. The council represents a major diplomatic and intellectual event, attracting scholars, theologians, and political figures from across Europe and the Byzantine Empire. Though the union achieved at the council would prove short-lived, the council's proceedings and the presence of Greek scholars significantly influenced Renaissance intellectual life.

sources.name: Council of Florence official acts
Contemporary chronicles

events.certainty_note: The council's convocation is well-documented in official records.

events.title: Cosimo de' Medici returns to Florence from exile
events.description: Cosimo de' Medici, the patriarch of the Medici banking family, returns to Florence after a year of exile (1433–1434). His return marks the beginning of the Medici family's rise to dominance in Florence, a position they would maintain for nearly three centuries. Cosimo's patronage of the arts, architecture, and learning would make Florence the cultural capital of the Renaissance. His political acumen and banking wealth allowed him to exercise influence without holding formal office, establishing a model of informal power that would influence European politics.

sources.name: Florentine chronicles (Vespasiano da Bisticci, Giovanni Villani)
Medici family records

events.certainty_note: Cosimo's return is well-documented in Florentine records.

events.title: Jan Žižka's death and Hussite Wars continue
events.description: Jan Žižka, the legendary Hussite military commander, dies in 1424 (not 1434; this entry corrects a potential confusion). By 1434, the Hussite Wars are in their final phase, with the radical Taborite faction being defeated by a coalition of moderate Hussites and Catholic forces. The wars, which began in 1419 following Jan Hus's execution, represent one of the earliest major religious conflicts in Christian Europe and establish Bohemia as a center of religious reform.

sources.name: Bohemian chronicles

events.certainty_note: The Hussite Wars' progression is well-documented, though Žižka died in 1424, not 1434.

events.title: Masaccio's death in Florence
events.description: Tommaso Cassai, known as Masaccio, dies in December 1428 (not 1434; this entry corrects a potential confusion). By 1434, his influence on Renaissance art is already profound. Masaccio's innovations in perspective, anatomical accuracy, and emotional depth in religious painting established new standards for Renaissance art. His frescoes in the Brancacci Chapel in Florence remain among the most influential works of the early Renaissance, studied by subsequent generations of artists including Michelangelo.

sources.name: Giorgio Vasari, Lives of the Artists
Brancacci Chapel frescoes (archaeological/artistic evidence)

events.certainty_note: Masaccio's death date is 1428, not 1434. His artistic legacy is well-documented.

events.title: Ming treasure voyages continue under Xuande Emperor
events.description: Zheng He's treasure voyages continue under the patronage of the Xuande Emperor. By 1434, the Ming fleet has completed multiple voyages to East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and Indian ports, demonstrating Chinese naval supremacy and diplomatic reach. These voyages represent the apex of Chinese maritime exploration before the Ming court's shift toward isolationism in the late 1430s.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Official Ming Records)
Ma Huan, Ying-yai Sheng-lan

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's voyages are well-documented in Ming court records.

disconfirming_evidence: This entry (1434-003) initially conflates Jan Žižka's death (1424) with the year 1434; the Hussite Wars continued after Žižka's death under other leaders. Entry 1434-004 similarly notes that Masaccio died in 1428, not 1434, though his influence persisted. These corrections highlight the importance of precise dating in historical research.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1434 is moderate. European political and cultural events are well-attested in chronicles and official records. Chinese court records are rich for the treasure voyages. The year 1434 is significant primarily as a waypoint in larger processes (the Council of Florence, the Medici rise, the Hussite Wars' conclusion, the treasure voyages' continuation) rather than as a year of singular events.

events.title: Zheng He's seventh treasure voyage departs from Nanjing
events.description: Admiral Zheng He departs Nanjing on his seventh and final treasure voyage, commanding a massive fleet of ships (possibly 300+ vessels) carrying diplomats, merchants, and military personnel. The voyage will visit East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and Indian ports before returning in 1436. This expedition represents the apex of Chinese maritime exploration and demonstrates Ming naval supremacy. The decision to cease these voyages after 1436 reflects factional politics at court and the prioritization of land-based defense against Mongol threats, marking a turning point in Chinese foreign policy.

events.title: Cosimo de' Medici exiled from Florence
events.description: Cosimo de' Medici, the patriarch of the Medici banking family, is exiled from Florence by the rival Albizzi faction, which fears the Medici family's growing wealth and influence. Cosimo is banished for one year (1433–1434), during which he travels to Venice and other cities. His exile is brief but significant: it demonstrates the political instability of Florence and foreshadows the Medici family's eventual rise to dominance. Upon his return in 1434, Cosimo's position is strengthened, and he becomes the de facto ruler of Florence.

sources.name: Florentine chronicles (Vespasiano da Bisticci, Giovanni Villani)

events.title: Council of Constance concludes; religious reform agenda set
events.description: The Council of Constance (1414–1418) concluded several years earlier, but its legacy continues to shape European Christianity in 1433. The council's decisions—including the condemnation of Jan Hus and the establishment of papal authority—set the agenda for religious reform that would dominate the 15th century. The Council of Florence (convened in 1434) represents a continuation of this reform effort, addressing church unity and the reconciliation of Eastern and Western Christianity.

events.title: Joan of Arc captured at Compiègne
events.description: Joan of Arc, the French military leader who rallied French forces during the Hundred Years' War, is captured by Burgundian forces at Compiègne on May 23, 1431 (not 1432; this entry corrects a potential confusion). By 1432, Joan is imprisoned and awaiting trial. Her capture marks a turning point in the Hundred Years' War, as French military momentum is disrupted. Joan's subsequent trial and execution in 1431 would make her a symbol of French national identity and Catholic martyrdom.

By 1432, major artistic and architectural projects are underway, including Brunelleschi's dome of the Florence Cathedral (completed 1436) and the early works of Donatello and Masaccio. The Medici family, though not yet dominant, is beginning to emerge as a major patron of the arts. The cultural efflorescence of Florence in the 1430s–1440s will establish the city as the intellectual and artistic capital of the Renaissance.

era_context: The early 15th century in Europe is marked by the Council of Constance's aftermath, the Hussite Wars in Bohemia, the early Italian Renaissance, and the consolidation of early modern nation-states. In East Asia, the Ming Dynasty under the Yongle Emperor's successors continues maritime exploration and administrative centralisation. The Ottoman Empire expands in the Balkans. This is a period of religious reformation, technological innovation (printing press emerging), and political fragmentation in Europe alongside cultural flourishing.

events.title: Execution of Joan of Arc
events.description: Joan of Arc, the French military leader who had rallied Valois forces during the Hundred Years' War, was burned at the stake on 30 May 1431 after being tried for heresy and witchcraft by an English-aligned ecclesiastical court. Her execution marked a symbolic turning point in Franco-English conflict, though the war continued for another two decades. Joan's death became a rallying point for French national identity and was later reversed by papal inquiry in 1456. Her trial records survive in detail, making this one of the best-documented executions of the medieval period.
key_figures: Henry VI (King of England, nominal authority)
Pierre Cauchon (Bishop of Beauvais, presiding judge)
Charles VII (King of France, did not intervene)

sources.name: Trial records of Joan of Arc (Procès de condamnation), contemporary court documents
Jean de Metz and Jean de Belleville, contemporary witnesses
Papal inquiry records (Procès en nullité), 1456

events.certainty_note: Execution date of 30 May 1431 is confirmed by multiple contemporary sources and trial records. The trial itself is exceptionally well-documented.
cross_references: 1456-XXX (Papal reversal of conviction, if included in dataset)

events.title: Council of Constance concludes; Hussite Wars intensify
events.description: The Council of Constance (1414–1418) had condemned Jan Hus and attempted to suppress Hussite reform. By 1431, the Hussite Wars were in their second decade, with radical Taborite and moderate Utraquist factions fighting Catholic forces and each other. The Council of Basel (1431–1449) convened partly to address the Hussite crisis. The conflict represented one of the earliest sustained challenges to papal authority and foreshadowed the Protestant Reformation by nearly a century. Military innovations, including the use of wagons as mobile fortifications, emerged from Hussite tactics.
key_figures: Jan Žižka (Hussite military commander, died 1424 but legacy dominant)
Sigismund of Luxembourg (Holy Roman Emperor, leading Catholic response)

sources.name: Laurence of Březová, Hussite Chronicle (contemporary account)
Council of Basel records and decrees
Catholic polemical accounts (e.g., Aeneas Silvius Piccolomini, later Pope Pius II)

events.certainty_note: The Council of Basel's opening and the ongoing Hussite Wars are well-documented. Specific battles and diplomatic efforts in 1431 are attested in contemporary chronicles.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure voyages continue under Ming Dynasty
events.region: Indian Ocean, Southeast Asia, East Africa
events.description: The Ming Dynasty's treasure voyages under Admiral Zheng He, which began in 1405, continued into the 1430s. The seventh and final voyage departed in 1433 and returned in 1435. These expeditions, involving massive fleets of hundreds of ships and tens of thousands of personnel, reached the coasts of East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and the Indian subcontinent. They were primarily diplomatic and trade missions rather than voyages of conquest. By 1431, the political climate in China was shifting; the Yongle Emperor (who sponsored the voyages) had died in 1424, and his successors were less enthusiastic about maritime expansion. The voyages would cease after 1435, marking a strategic pivot toward continental consolidation.
key_figures: Xuande Emperor (reigning 1426–1435, less supportive of voyages)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Ming Veritable Records), official court records
Ma Huan, Ying-yai Sheng-lan (Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores), eyewitness account
Archaeological evidence from shipwrecks and port sites

events.certainty_note: The voyages are well-documented in Ming records and eyewitness accounts. The seventh voyage's dates (1433–1435) are confirmed.

events.title: Medici consolidation in Florence; Cosimo de' Medici's influence grows
events.description: By 1431, Cosimo de' Medici (1389–1464), the patriarch of the Medici banking family, had become the dominant political figure in Florence despite holding no formal office. His influence grew through patronage, banking wealth, and strategic alliances. The Medici family's rise was foundational to the Italian Renaissance, as they became major patrons of art, architecture, and humanist scholarship. In 1431, Florence was consolidating its territorial control in Tuscany and establishing itself as a major power in Italian politics, rivalling Venice and Milan.
key_figures: Cosimo de' Medici (banker, de facto ruler)

sources.name: Vespasiano da Bisticci, Lives of Illustrious Men (later compilation, but based on contemporary knowledge)
Medici family correspondence and account books
Florentine government records (Signoria)

events.certainty_note: Cosimo's growing influence by 1431 is attested in family records and government documents, though the precise chronology of his rise to dominance is debated by historians. The year 1431 marks a point in his consolidation rather than a single decisive event.

events.title: Portuguese maritime expansion; Prince Henry the Navigator's school
events.description: Prince Henry of Portugal (1394–1460), known as 'the Navigator,' was actively sponsoring maritime expeditions along the African coast in the 1430s. His school at Sagres (founded c. 1418) was training navigators and cartographers. By 1431, Portuguese ships had explored the Canary Islands and were beginning systematic exploration of the West African coast. This marked the beginning of European oceanic exploration that would lead to the Age of Discovery. Henry's patronage was driven by commercial interest (gold, spices, slaves) and religious zeal (crusade against Islam).
key_figures: Henry the Navigator (Prince of Portugal, patron)

sources.name: Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery and Conquest of Guinea (mid-15th century, but based on contemporary records)
Portuguese royal records and navigation logs
Cartographic evidence (portolan charts)

events.certainty_note: Henry's patronage and the school's activities are well-documented, though specific expeditions in 1431 are less precisely dated. The broader context of Portuguese exploration in the 1430s is confirmed.

disconfirming_evidence: Joan of Arc's trial has been extensively re-examined by modern scholarship. While her execution on 30 May 1431 is confirmed, historians debate the extent to which she was a genuine military strategist versus a symbolic figurehead exploited by French nobles. Some scholars argue that the military successes attributed to her (particularly at Orléans in 1429) were primarily due to French commanders like Jean de Dunois and that Joan's role was propagandistic. Additionally, the trial itself is now understood as a political show trial orchestrated by English and Burgundian interests, not a genuine heresy inquiry. The 'Hussite Wars' framing can obscure the fact that Hussite factions were as much in conflict with each other as with Catholic forces; the Taborites and Utraquists had fundamentally different visions. The narrative of Zheng He's voyages as 'peaceful exploration' has been complicated by recent scholarship emphasizing their role in Ming tributary diplomacy and the coercive aspects of 'gift-giving' to foreign rulers. The characterization of these voyages as 'treasure voyages' is partly a modern invention; Ming sources call them 'treasure ship voyages' (baochuan), but the term 'treasure' may be misleading—the ships carried diplomatic gifts and trade goods, not plundered wealth.
historiographic_note: The year 1431 is well-documented for Western Europe (Joan of Arc's trial is exceptionally detailed), moderately documented for East Asia (Ming records are comprehensive), and sparsely documented for most other regions. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history are underrepresented. Joan of Arc's trial records are among the most complete medieval trial documents, but they reflect the biases of her English and Burgundian judges. Ming records are official court documents, which tend to emphasize imperial achievements and downplay failures or dissent. Portuguese exploration records are fragmentary and often compiled retrospectively. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Southeast Asia have minimal written documentation for this year; oral traditions and archaeological evidence are not systematically integrated into Western historical narratives. The dominance of European sources in modern historical training means that events in other regions are likely underrepresented in this summary.

events.title: Capture of Joan of Arc at Compiègne
events.description: On 23 May 1430, Joan of Arc was captured by Burgundian forces during a military engagement at Compiègne. She had been leading French forces in an attempt to relieve the besieged town. Her capture was a turning point in the Hundred Years' War and in her own fate. Joan was initially held by Burgundian forces but was soon sold to the English, who held her for trial on charges of heresy and witchcraft. Her capture effectively ended her active military career and set in motion the events leading to her execution in 1431.
key_figures: Philip the Good (Duke of Burgundy, negotiated sale to English)

sources.name: Jean de Metz, contemporary account of Joan's capture
Burgundian court records
English government records

events.certainty_note: The capture date of 23 May 1430 is confirmed by multiple contemporary sources. The location and circumstances are well-documented.

events.title: Hussite victory at Domažlice; Catholic crusade repelled
events.description: On 14 August 1430, Hussite forces under Prokop the Great defeated a Catholic crusade army at Domažlice. This was one of the most significant Hussite military victories, demonstrating the effectiveness of their wagon-fortress tactics and the weakness of the Catholic response. The victory boosted Hussite morale and led to negotiations that would eventually result in the Compacts of Prague (1436), which granted some Hussite demands. The battle exemplified the military innovations of the Hussite Wars, which influenced European warfare for centuries.
key_figures: Sigismund of Luxembourg (Holy Roman Emperor, leading Catholic response)

sources.name: Laurence of Březová, Hussite Chronicle
Catholic accounts (e.g., Aeneas Silvius Piccolomini)
Bohemian government records

events.certainty_note: The battle of Domažlice on 14 August 1430 is well-documented in contemporary Hussite and Catholic sources. The outcome and significance are confirmed.

events.title: Brunelleschi completes the dome of Florence Cathedral
events.description: Filippo Brunelleschi completed the construction of the dome of the Florence Cathedral (the Duomo) in 1430 or 1431 (sources vary slightly on the exact date). The dome was an engineering marvel, constructed without a wooden framework using an innovative double-shell design and herringbone brick pattern. Its completion was a symbol of Florentine civic pride and technical achievement. The dome became one of the most iconic structures of the Renaissance and demonstrated the revival of classical architectural principles. The project had begun in 1420 and took over a decade to complete.
key_figures: Lorenzo Ghiberti (co-architect, though Brunelleschi was primary)
Cosimo de' Medici (patron, indirectly through civic support)

sources.name: Vasari, Lives of the Artists (later compilation, but based on contemporary knowledge)
Florentine government records (Opera del Duomo)
Brunelleschi's own writings and designs
Archaeological and architectural analysis

events.certainty_note: The dome's completion is traditionally dated to 1436, but some sources place it in 1430 or 1431. The exact completion date is debated by architectural historians, though the project's general timeline is confirmed.

events.title: Xuande Emperor's reign in Ming China; cultural patronage
events.description: The Xuande Emperor (r. 1426–1435) was actively patronizing the arts and literature in 1430. His reign is considered a golden age of Ming culture, with significant achievements in porcelain production (Xuande blue-and-white porcelain is highly prized), painting, and calligraphy. The emperor himself was an accomplished painter and calligrapher. The Xuande period saw the compilation of the Yongle Encyclopedia (completed 1408, but continued to be copied and distributed) and the production of high-quality imperial artworks. This was also the period of Zheng He's final voyages.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Ming Veritable Records)
Xuande porcelain and artwork (material evidence)
Later Ming histories and compilations

events.certainty_note: The Xuande Emperor's reign and his cultural patronage are well-documented in Ming records. The production of Xuande porcelain and artwork is confirmed by material evidence.

events.title: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans; siege of Janina
events.description: The Ottoman Empire, under Murad II, was expanding in the Balkans in the 1430s. In 1430, the Ottomans besieged and captured the city of Janina (Ioannina) in what is now Greece. This was part of a broader Ottoman campaign to consolidate control over the Balkans and eliminate remaining Byzantine and Venetian strongholds. The capture of Janina was a significant step in Ottoman expansion and marked the decline of Venetian influence in the region. The city had been an important commercial and cultural center.

sources.name: Ottoman chronicles (e.g., Ashikpashazade)
Venetian government records and correspondence
Byzantine sources (fragmentary)

events.certainty_note: The capture of Janina in 1430 is attested in Ottoman and Venetian sources, though the exact date may be 1429 or 1431 depending on the source. The event itself is confirmed.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Brunelleschi's dome completion is disputed. While 1430 or 1431 is commonly cited, some sources place the completion in 1436, with the consecration occurring in 1436. The exact date of the dome's structural completion versus its ceremonial completion is unclear. The capture of Janina is traditionally dated to 1430, but some Ottoman sources suggest 1429, and Venetian records are ambiguous. The significance of the Hussite victory at Domažlice has been somewhat overstated in popular accounts; while it was a significant military success, it did not immediately lead to a resolution of the Hussite Wars, and the Compacts of Prague (1436) represented a compromise rather than a Hussite victory. The characterization of the Xuande period as a 'golden age' reflects a retrospective aesthetic judgment rather than a contemporary assessment; the emperor's reign was also marked by political tensions and the beginning of the decline of maritime exploration.
historiographic_note: The year 1430 is well-documented for Western Europe (Joan of Arc's capture is extensively recorded) and moderately documented for East Asia (Ming records are comprehensive). Ottoman sources are less detailed for this period, and Venetian records, while present, are fragmentary. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic and social history are underrepresented. Joan of Arc's capture is recorded from multiple perspectives (French, English, Burgundian), but each source reflects the biases of its origin. The Hussite Wars are documented in both Hussite and Catholic sources, which often contradict each other on details and significance. Ming records are official court documents that emphasize imperial achievements. Ottoman sources are often compiled retrospectively and may conflate events from different years. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Southeast Asia have minimal written documentation for this year.

events.title: Joan of Arc's military victories; Relief of Orléans
events.region: Orléans, Loire Valley, Kingdom of France
events.description: In 1429, Joan of Arc led French forces to a series of military victories that marked a turning point in the Hundred Years' War. Most notably, she participated in the relief of Orléans, which had been under English siege. The French victory at Orléans on 8 May 1429 was a major morale boost and demonstrated that English forces could be defeated. Joan's subsequent victories at Jargeau (12 June), Meung-sur-Loire (15 June), and Beaugency (17 June) further weakened English control of the Loire Valley. These victories led to the coronation of Charles VII at Reims on 17 July 1429, legitimizing his claim to the French throne. Joan's role in these victories is debated by historians; some emphasize her military leadership, while others argue she was primarily a symbolic figurehead whose presence boosted morale.
key_figures: Charles VII (King of France, crowned at Reims)
Jean de Dunois (French commander, 'the Bastard of Orléans')

sources.name: Jean de Metz, contemporary account
Enguerrand de Monstrelet, Chronicle (near-contemporary)
English government records and military reports
French royal records

events.certainty_note: The major battles and Joan's participation are confirmed by multiple contemporary sources. The dates of the battles are well-established. The significance of Joan's role is debated, but the events themselves are confirmed.

events.title: Coronation of Charles VII at Reims
events.description: On 17 July 1429, Charles VII was crowned King of France at Reims Cathedral. This coronation was of immense symbolic and political significance. It legitimized Charles's claim to the French throne against the English-backed Henry VI and marked a turning point in the Hundred Years' War. The coronation was made possible by Joan of Arc's military victories, which had opened the route to Reims. The ceremony itself followed traditional French coronation rituals and was attended by French nobility. The coronation boosted French morale and international recognition of Charles VII's legitimacy.
key_figures: Joan of Arc (military leader who enabled the coronation)

sources.name: French royal records and chronicles
Jean de Metz, contemporary account
Enguerrand de Monstrelet, Chronicle

events.certainty_note: The coronation date of 17 July 1429 is confirmed by multiple contemporary sources. The ceremony and its significance are well-documented.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure voyages; sixth voyage returns
events.region: Indian Ocean, East Africa, Arabian Peninsula
events.description: The sixth of Zheng He's treasure voyages returned to China in 1429 after departing in 1421. These massive expeditions, involving hundreds of ships and tens of thousands of personnel, reached the coasts of East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and the Indian subcontinent. The voyages were primarily diplomatic and trade missions, establishing tributary relationships and conducting trade. By 1429, the political climate in China was shifting; the Yongle Emperor, who had sponsored the voyages, had died in 1424. His successors were less enthusiastic about maritime expansion, and the voyages would cease after 1435. The return of the sixth voyage in 1429 marked the beginning of the end of this era of Chinese maritime exploration.
key_figures: Xuande Emperor (reigning 1426–1435, less supportive than his predecessor)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Ming Veritable Records)
Ma Huan, Ying-yai Sheng-lan (Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores)
Archaeological evidence from shipwrecks and port sites

events.certainty_note: The return of the sixth voyage in 1429 is attested in Ming records, though the exact date may vary slightly depending on the source. The broader context of the voyages is confirmed.

events.title: Hussite Wars; continued conflict in Bohemia
events.description: The Hussite Wars continued in 1429 with ongoing conflict between Hussite factions (Taborites and Utraquists) and Catholic forces. The year saw various military campaigns and skirmishes, though no single decisive battle. The Hussite movement, which had begun as a religious reform movement following the execution of Jan Hus in 1415, had evolved into a complex military and political conflict. By 1429, the Hussite forces had demonstrated their military effectiveness through innovations in wagon-fortress tactics and the use of firearms. The conflict would continue until the Compacts of Prague in 1436.
key_figures: Sigismund of Luxembourg (Holy Roman Emperor, leading Catholic response)
Jan Žižka (Hussite commander, died 1424 but legacy dominant)

sources.name: Laurence of Březová, Hussite Chronicle
Catholic polemical accounts

events.certainty_note: The continuation of the Hussite Wars in 1429 is confirmed by contemporary sources. Specific battles and campaigns are documented, though the exact chronology may vary.

events.title: Portuguese maritime exploration; expansion along African coast
events.description: Prince Henry of Portugal (1394–1460) continued to sponsor maritime expeditions along the African coast in 1429. Portuguese ships were systematically exploring the West African coast, seeking gold, spices, and other valuable commodities. The expeditions were also motivated by religious zeal (crusade against Islam) and the desire to establish trading posts. By 1429, Portuguese explorers had reached the coast of what is now Senegal and Guinea. These early expeditions laid the groundwork for the Portuguese slave trade, which would intensify in the following decades.
key_figures: Henry the Navigator (Prince of Portugal, patron)

sources.name: Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery and Conquest of Guinea (mid-15th century, but based on contemporary records)
Portuguese royal records and navigation logs
Cartographic evidence (portolan charts)

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration of the African coast in 1429 is attested in later chronicles and cartographic evidence. Specific expeditions in 1429 are less precisely dated than the broader context of exploration.

disconfirming_evidence: The role of Joan of Arc in the military victories of 1429 is heavily debated by modern historians. While her participation is confirmed, the extent of her military leadership versus her symbolic value is disputed. Some scholars argue that the victories were primarily due to French commanders like Jean de Dunois and that Joan's presence was exploited for propaganda purposes. The coronation of Charles VII at Reims, while symbolically important, did not immediately end the Hundred Years' War; the conflict continued for another 24 years. The characterization of Zheng He's voyages as 'peaceful exploration' obscures their role in Ming tributary diplomacy and the coercive aspects of 'gift-giving' to foreign rulers. The Hussite Wars are often portrayed as a conflict between 'reformers' and 'conservatives,' but this oversimplifies the complex factional divisions within the Hussite movement itself. Portuguese exploration is often presented as a precursor to the Age of Discovery, but this teleological framing obscures the fact that Portuguese expansion was driven primarily by commercial and religious motives, not by a desire to 'discover' new lands.
historiographic_note: The year 1429 is well-documented for Western Europe (Joan of Arc's campaigns are extensively recorded) and moderately documented for East Asia (Ming records are comprehensive). The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history are underrepresented. Joan of Arc's campaigns are recorded from multiple perspectives (French, English, Burgundian), but each source reflects the biases of its origin. The French sources tend to emphasize Joan's role, while English sources downplay it. Ming records are official court documents that emphasize imperial achievements. Portuguese exploration records are fragmentary and often compiled retrospectively. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Southeast Asia have minimal written documentation for this year. The dominance of European sources in modern historical training means that events in other regions are likely underrepresented.

events.title: English siege of Orléans begins
events.region: Orléans, Loire Valley, Kingdom of France
events.description: In 1428, English forces under the command of William de la Pole and John Talbot began a siege of the city of Orléans, a key stronghold in the Loire Valley. The siege would last until 1429 and become one of the most famous sieges of the Hundred Years' War. Orléans was strategically important because it controlled access to the Loire Valley and was a major French stronghold. The English siege was part of a broader strategy to consolidate English control over France. The siege would be broken by French forces under Joan of Arc in 1429, marking a turning point in the war.
key_figures: Jean de Dunois (French commander, 'the Bastard of Orléans')

sources.name: English government records and military reports

events.title: Hussite Wars; Battle of Tachov
events.description: In 1428, Hussite forces engaged in military campaigns against Catholic forces and rival Hussite factions. The Battle of Tachov (also known as the Battle of Tachau) in 1428 was one of several engagements in the ongoing Hussite Wars. The Hussite forces, under commanders like Prokop the Great, continued to demonstrate military effectiveness through their innovative tactics, including the use of wagon-fortresses and early firearms. The conflict remained unresolved, with neither side achieving a decisive victory.
key_figures: Sigismund of Luxembourg (Holy Roman Emperor, leading Catholic response)

events.title: Xuande Emperor's reign in Ming China; administrative consolidation
events.description: The Xuande Emperor (r. 1426–1435) was consolidating Ming administrative control in 1428. His reign is characterized by efforts to strengthen central authority, improve bureaucratic efficiency, and maintain the stability achieved under his predecessor, the Yongle Emperor. The Xuande period saw the continuation of Zheng He's treasure voyages, though the emperor was less enthusiastic about maritime expansion than his predecessor. The emperor was also an accomplished artist and patron of the arts, contributing to the cultural flourishing of the Ming Dynasty.

events.title: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans; consolidation of power
events.description: The Ottoman Empire under Murad II (r. 1421–1451) was consolidating its control over the Balkans in 1428. The Ottomans were expanding their territorial holdings and eliminating remaining Byzantine and Venetian strongholds. This period saw the Ottoman military machine becoming increasingly effective, with the development of the Janissary corps and the use of gunpowder weapons. The Ottoman expansion was part of a broader process that would culminate in the fall of Constantinople in 1453.

events.title: Hundred Years' War; English dominance in France
events.description: In 1427, the Hundred Years' War was in its final phase, with English forces still dominant in France but facing increasing French resistance. The English controlled much of northern France, including Normandy and parts of the Loire Valley. The war had been ongoing since 1337 and would continue until 1453. In 1427, the military situation was relatively stable, with neither side achieving decisive victories. The English were consolidating their control, while the French were preparing for the military campaigns that would begin in 1429 under Joan of Arc's leadership. The war was economically draining for both sides and was a major factor in the political instability of the period.

The Hussite Wars are often portrayed as a conflict between 'reformers' and 'conservatives,' but this oversimplifies the complex factional divisions within the Hussite movement. The Xuande Emperor's reign is often characterized as a 'golden age,' but this reflects a retrospective aesthetic judgment rather than a contemporary assessment; the emperor's reign was also marked by political tensions and the beginning of the decline of maritime exploration. Ottoman expansion in the Balkans is often presented as inevitable, but this obscures the resistance of local populations and the contingency of Ottoman military successes. Portuguese exploration is often presented as a precursor to the Age of Discovery, but this teleological framing obscures the fact that Portuguese expansion was driven primarily by commercial and religious motives.
historiographic_note: The year 1427 is moderately documented for Western Europe (the Hundred Years' War is well-recorded) and moderately documented for East Asia (Ming records are comprehensive). Ottoman sources are less detailed for this period, and Venetian records are fragmentary. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history are underrepresented. English and French sources on the Hundred Years' War reflect the biases of their origins. Ming records are official court documents that emphasize imperial achievements. Ottoman sources are often compiled retrospectively. Portuguese exploration records are fragmentary and often compiled retrospectively. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Southeast Asia have minimal written documentation for this year.

In Europe, the Council of Constance has recently concluded (1418), and the Hussite Wars continue in Bohemia. The Ottoman Empire is expanding in the Balkans and Eastern Mediterranean. The Age of Exploration is beginning, with Portuguese expeditions probing the African coast.

events.title: Death of the Yongle Emperor; Accession of the Hongwu Emperor's Grandson
events.description: The Yongle Emperor died in 1424, but 1426 marks the consolidation of power under his successor in the context of Ming court politics. The transition involved significant factional struggles between eunuchs and scholar-officials. This period saw the continuation of the Forbidden City's completion and the establishment of Ming administrative structures that would define the dynasty for two centuries.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Official Ming court records

events.certainty_note: Ming dynastic records are well-preserved and detailed. The Xuande Emperor's reign is well-documented.

events.title: Zheng He's Seventh Treasure Voyage Expedition
events.region: Indian Ocean, East Africa, Arabian Peninsula
events.description: Admiral Zheng He, the eunuch commander of the Ming treasure fleets, embarked on his seventh and final major voyage (1433–1435 in some sources, but preparations and earlier phases occurred in 1426). These massive expeditions, involving hundreds of ships and tens of thousands of personnel, reached the coasts of East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and the Indian Ocean. The voyages demonstrated Ming naval supremacy and diplomatic reach but were eventually curtailed due to cost and factional opposition at court.

sources.name: Ming Shilu
Luo Maodeng, The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores (Yingya Shenglan), compiled early 15th century
Archaeological evidence from shipwrecks and coastal sites

events.certainty_note: The exact dates of Zheng He's voyages are debated by scholars. Some sources place the seventh voyage in 1433–1435; others suggest earlier phases in 1426. Contemporary documentation is limited; most records are from later compilations.

events.title: Hussite Wars Continue in Bohemia
events.description: The Hussite Wars, sparked by the execution of Jan Hus in 1415, continued throughout the 1420s. In 1426, various Hussite factions—including the radical Taborites and the more moderate Utraquists—engaged in military campaigns against Catholic forces and each other. These conflicts were driven by religious reform demands and social upheaval. The wars would not fully conclude until the 1430s, reshaping Central European religious and political landscapes.

sources.name: Laurentius of Březová, Chronicle of the Hussite Wars
Aeneas Silvius Piccolomini, Historia Bohemica

events.certainty_note: Contemporary chronicles document the Hussite Wars in detail. Dates and major engagements are well-attested.
cross_references: 1434-001 (Battle of Lipany, end of radical Hussite phase)

events.title: Portuguese Exploration of the African Coast
events.description: Under the patronage of Prince Henry the Navigator, Portuguese expeditions continued systematic exploration of the West African coast in the 1420s. By 1426, Portuguese ships had reached beyond Cape Bojador (crossed in 1434 in some sources, but expeditions were ongoing). These voyages were motivated by trade, gold, and religious conversion. They marked the beginning of European maritime expansion and the eventual transatlantic slave trade.
key_figures: Portuguese explorers and navigators (names often unrecorded)

sources.name: Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery of Guinea
Portuguese royal records and navigation logs

events.certainty_note: The exact timeline of early Portuguese exploration is uncertain. Zurara's chronicle, written decades later, is the primary source but contains legendary elements. Archaeological and archival evidence supports ongoing exploration in this period.
cross_references: 1488-001 (Bartolomeu Dias rounds Cape of Good Hope)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating and significance of Zheng He's seventh voyage is disputed. Some scholars argue the voyages ended earlier (1433) or that the 1426 date conflates multiple expeditions. Additionally, the scale and purpose of these voyages have been reinterpreted: while traditionally celebrated as demonstrations of Ming power, recent scholarship emphasizes their diplomatic and tributary functions rather than exploration for its own sake. The Portuguese exploration narrative has been complicated by postcolonial scholarship, which notes that the 'Age of Exploration' framing obscures the agency and sophistication of African and Asian maritime societies.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1426 is heavily weighted toward East Asia (Ming China) and Europe (Bohemia, Portugal), reflecting the survival and accessibility of written records in these regions. Chinese sources are abundant but often compiled retrospectively. European sources are contemporary but limited to elite political and military events. African, American, and Oceanic histories are largely absent from written records of this year, not because nothing happened but because documentation was not preserved or has not been systematically studied. The 'significance' of events listed reflects historiographic bias toward literate, centralized states.

events.title: Transition in Ming Leadership; Xuande Emperor's Early Reign
events.description: The year 1425 marks the early consolidation of the Xuande Emperor's reign (formally began 1426, but preparations and power consolidation occurred in 1425). The Xuande Emperor was known for his support of the arts, particularly painting and calligraphy, and for his pragmatic approach to eunuch power and scholar-official factionalism. His reign is often regarded as a golden age of Ming cultural achievement.

sources.name: Ming Shilu
Official Ming court records

events.certainty_note: Ming records are well-preserved. The Xuande Emperor's reign is thoroughly documented.

events.title: Hussite Military Campaigns in Bohemia
events.description: In 1425, Hussite forces under commanders like Prokop the Great continued military operations against Catholic forces and rival factions. The Taborite faction, known for radical religious and social demands, engaged in aggressive campaigns. These wars were characterized by innovative military tactics, including the use of wagon fortifications (Wagenburg), which influenced European military strategy for centuries.

sources.name: Laurentius of Březová, Chronicle of the Hussite Wars
Contemporary accounts from Catholic chroniclers

events.certainty_note: Contemporary chronicles provide detailed accounts of Hussite military campaigns.

events.title: Early Italian Renaissance: Humanist Scholarship in Florence
events.description: In 1425, Florence was a center of early Renaissance humanist scholarship. Figures like Leonardo Bruni and Poggio Bracciolini were engaged in recovering and studying classical texts. The Medici family's patronage of the arts and learning was beginning to shape Florence's cultural dominance. This period saw the revival of classical learning that would define the Renaissance.

sources.name: Leonardo Bruni, Historiae Florentini Populi
Correspondence and records of humanist scholars
Medici family records

events.certainty_note: Humanist scholarship in Florence is well-documented through surviving correspondence and published works.

events.title: Ottoman Expansion in the Balkans
events.description: The Ottoman Empire continued its expansion in the Balkans during the 1420s. Ottoman forces under Murad II were consolidating control over former Byzantine and Serbian territories. This period saw the gradual encirclement of Constantinople, which would fall in 1453. Ottoman military organization and gunpowder technology were advancing, making them a formidable power in the Eastern Mediterranean.

sources.name: Ottoman chronicles and administrative records
Byzantine and Venetian sources

events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in the Balkans is well-documented in multiple contemporary sources.

events.title: Portuguese Maritime Expeditions Continue
events.description: Portuguese expeditions under Prince Henry the Navigator continued probing the West African coast. These voyages were part of a systematic program of exploration and trade. By 1425, Portuguese knowledge of African geography and trade routes was expanding, laying the groundwork for later major discoveries.

sources.name: Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery of Guinea
Portuguese royal records

events.certainty_note: The exact timeline of early Portuguese exploration is uncertain. Most detailed accounts come from later chronicles.
cross_references: 1488-001 (Bartolomeu Dias rounds Cape of Good Hope)

disconfirming_evidence: The characterization of the Xuande Emperor's reign as a 'golden age' has been nuanced by recent scholarship, which emphasizes the continuation of factional struggles and the eventual curtailment of Zheng He's voyages due to court opposition. The Italian Renaissance narrative has been complicated by postcolonial and global history perspectives, which note that 'Renaissance' was a European phenomenon and that sophisticated intellectual and artistic traditions were flourishing simultaneously in China, the Islamic world, and elsewhere. The Ottoman expansion narrative, while confirmed, has sometimes been presented as inevitable or unidirectional, obscuring the complex political and military dynamics of the period.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1425 is similar to 1426: heavily weighted toward East Asia and Europe, with minimal coverage of Africa, the Americas, and other regions. Chinese sources are abundant but retrospective. European sources are contemporary but elite-focused. The 'significance' of events reflects historiographic bias toward literate, centralized states and toward regions with surviving written records.
