events.title: Death of the Yongle Emperor
events.description: The Yongle Emperor, one of the most powerful and expansionist Ming rulers, died in 1424 (some sources give 1424, others 1425; the exact date is debated). His reign (1402–1424) was marked by military campaigns, the construction of the Forbidden City, the compilation of the Yongle Encyclopedia, and the sponsorship of Zheng He's treasure voyages. His death marked a transition in Ming policy, with subsequent rulers being less expansionist and more focused on consolidation and defense.

sources.name: Ming Shilu
Official Ming court records

events.certainty_note: The Yongle Emperor's death is well-documented in Ming records, though the exact date (1424 vs. 1425) varies by source.

events.title: Hussite Wars: Early Phase in Bohemia
events.description: The Hussite Wars, sparked by the execution of Jan Hus in 1415, were in their early and most radical phase in 1424. The Taborite faction, a radical Hussite group, was engaged in military campaigns against Catholic forces and rival Hussite factions. The year 1424 saw significant military activity, including the Battle of Malešov. These wars were driven by religious reform demands and social upheaval, and they would reshape Central European religious and political landscapes.

events.title: Death of Jan Žižka, Hussite Military Commander
events.description: Jan Žižka, the legendary Hussite military commander, died in 1424 (some sources give October 1424). Žižka was blind but led Hussite forces to numerous victories against Catholic armies. His death marked a transition in Hussite military leadership, with Prokop the Great assuming command. Žižka's innovations in military tactics, particularly the use of wagon fortifications, influenced European warfare for centuries.

sources.name: Laurentius of Březová, Chronicle of the Hussite Wars

events.certainty_note: Žižka's death in 1424 is well-documented in contemporary sources.

events.title: Ottoman Consolidation Under Murad II
events.description: Murad II, who became Ottoman Sultan in 1421, was consolidating Ottoman power in Anatolia and the Balkans in 1424. His reign saw the suppression of internal rebellions and the expansion of Ottoman control over former Byzantine and Serbian territories. Murad II's military and administrative reforms strengthened the Ottoman state, making it a major regional power.

The significance of his reign has been reinterpreted by recent scholarship, which emphasizes the costs of his military campaigns and the eventual curtailment of his expansionist policies by his successors. Jan Žižka's legendary status has been somewhat mythologized; while his military innovations were significant, some accounts exaggerate his personal role and underestimate the contributions of other Hussite commanders. The Portuguese exploration narrative has been complicated by postcolonial scholarship, which notes that early Portuguese expeditions were often unsuccessful and that the 'Age of Exploration' framing obscures the agency and sophistication of African societies.

events.title: Zheng He's Seventh Treasure Voyage departs from Nanjing
events.description: Admiral Zheng He (鄭和) embarks on his seventh and final major maritime expedition from Nanjing, commanding a massive fleet of treasure ships (baochuan). The expedition will visit East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and the Indian Ocean littoral, demonstrating Ming naval supremacy and tributary diplomacy. This voyage represents the apex of early 15th-century maritime technology and Chinese naval power, though it will be curtailed after the Yongle Emperor's death in 1424. The expeditions served diplomatic, commercial, and intelligence-gathering purposes, establishing Ming prestige across the Indian Ocean world.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Luo Maodeng, The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores (Yingya Shenglan), compiled early 15th century

events.certainty_note: Departure date and voyage sequence are well-documented in Ming imperial records and contemporary chronicles. The exact dating of the seventh voyage's start is sometimes placed in 1421 or 1422 depending on source; 1421 is the consensus date.
cross_references: 1424-001 (Yongle Emperor's death, leading to expedition curtailment)

events.title: Hussite forces defeat Catholic crusade at Battle of Kutná Hora
events.description: Hussite military forces, led by Jan Žižka's successors, defeat a Catholic crusade army near Kutná Hora in central Bohemia. The Hussites, followers of the executed reformer Jan Hus, have been conducting a sustained military and religious resistance against Catholic and imperial forces since 1419. This victory consolidates Hussite control over much of Bohemia and demonstrates the effectiveness of their innovative military tactics, including the use of wagon fortifications (tabor). The conflict reflects deep religious and social tensions within the Holy Roman Empire.
key_figures: Sigismund of Hungary (Holy Roman Emperor, leading crusade)

sources.name: Laurence of Březová, Chronicle of the Hussite Wars
Historiographic consensus on Hussite military campaigns

events.certainty_note: The battle is attested in Hussite chronicles, though exact dating and casualty figures vary between sources. The year 1421 is conventional; some sources place it in 1422.

events.title: Forbidden City construction nears completion in Beijing
events.description: The construction of the Forbidden City (紫禁城), the imperial palace complex in Beijing, approaches completion under the Yongle Emperor's direction. Work began in 1406 and will be largely finished by 1420, with final refinements continuing through 1421. The complex represents an extraordinary feat of architectural and logistical coordination, employing hundreds of thousands of workers and incorporating advanced timber-frame construction, drainage systems, and decorative arts. The Forbidden City becomes the symbolic and administrative center of Ming power and will remain so for nearly five centuries.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Archaeological and architectural evidence from the Forbidden City

events.certainty_note: Construction dates and phases are well-documented in Ming records. The complex was functionally complete by 1420, with 1421 marking final phases.

events.title: Portuguese exploration of West African coast continues under Henry the Navigator
events.description: Portuguese maritime expeditions, sponsored by Prince Henry the Navigator (Henrique o Navegador), continue systematic exploration of the West African coast. By 1421, Portuguese ships have reached and begun establishing trading posts along the Guinea coast. These voyages represent the beginning of European maritime expansion and the Atlantic slave trade, driven by commercial interest in gold, spices, and enslaved people. The expeditions employ improved navigation technology (astrolabe, caravel design) and establish a pattern of coastal fortification and trade monopoly that will characterize European colonialism.
key_figures: Portuguese sea captains and navigators (names often not recorded)

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Zheng He's voyages has been subject to historiographic revision. While traditionally celebrated as evidence of Chinese maritime superiority and global reach, modern scholarship emphasizes that these expeditions were ultimately discontinued and did not lead to sustained colonial expansion or trade networks comparable to later European ventures. Some scholars argue the voyages were primarily diplomatic and tributary in nature rather than commercially driven, and that their termination after 1424 reflects a deliberate Ming policy shift toward continental consolidation rather than maritime dominance. The Forbidden City's construction involved massive labor mobilization; modern scholarship has questioned earlier estimates of worker mortality and conditions, though documentation remains limited.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1421 is relatively rich for Ming China and Europe, but heavily skewed toward elite political and military events. The record is dominated by court chronicles, administrative records, and later compilations by scholars with access to imperial archives. African, American, and Asian sources outside the Ming sphere are underrepresented in surviving records accessible to modern scholarship. The Portuguese exploration record is filtered through later hagiographic chronicles (Zurara) written decades after events. Oral traditions and non-elite perspectives are largely absent.

events.title: Yongle Emperor moves Ming court to Beijing; Forbidden City becomes imperial residence
events.description: The Yongle Emperor formally relocates the Ming imperial court from Nanjing to Beijing (Peking), establishing the Forbidden City as the primary imperial residence and administrative center. This move represents a strategic shift toward the north, closer to the Great Wall and the Mongol frontier, and signals Ming consolidation of power over all of China. The relocation required massive infrastructure development, including the completion of the Grand Canal extension to supply the northern capital. Beijing becomes the symbolic and political heart of the Ming Dynasty and will remain the capital of China for nearly five centuries.
key_figures: Officials and architects overseeing the relocation

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The relocation and establishment of Beijing as the capital in 1420 is well-documented in Ming imperial records. The Forbidden City was functionally complete by this date.

events.title: Hussite forces consolidate control over Bohemia
events.description: Hussite military forces, under the continued leadership of Jan Žižka and his successors, consolidate control over much of Bohemia following a series of victories against Catholic and imperial forces. The Hussites have successfully defended their religious and political autonomy against multiple crusades launched by the Holy Roman Emperor Sigismund. By 1420, the Hussite movement has evolved into a powerful military and political force, with sophisticated tactics (wagon fortifications, combined arms) and strong popular support. The conflict reflects deep religious reform sentiment and social tensions within the Holy Roman Empire.
key_figures: Sigismund of Hungary (Holy Roman Emperor, opponent)

sources.name: Laurence of Březová, Chronicle of the Hussite Wars

events.certainty_note: The consolidation of Hussite control by 1420 is well-attested in contemporary chronicles and modern scholarship.

events.title: Zheng He's sixth treasure voyage returns to China
events.description: Admiral Zheng He's sixth major maritime expedition returns to Nanjing after visiting East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and ports throughout the Indian Ocean. The voyage demonstrates the continued scale and sophistication of Ming maritime operations, with fleets of hundreds of ships and tens of thousands of personnel. These expeditions serve diplomatic, commercial, and intelligence-gathering purposes, establishing Ming tributary relationships and prestige across the Indian Ocean world. The voyages represent the apex of early 15th-century maritime technology and Chinese naval power.

events.title: Council of Constance concludes; Jan Hus's legacy remains contested
events.description: The Council of Constance, which had condemned Jan Hus to death in 1415, concludes its sessions. The council's decisions regarding religious reform and church authority remain controversial, particularly in Bohemia, where Hus's followers continue to resist papal authority and advocate for communion in both kinds (sub utraque specie) and other reforms. The council's failure to address the underlying grievances that motivated Hus's reform movement contributes to the continuation of the Hussite Wars and the broader fragmentation of religious authority in Central Europe.
key_figures: Emperor Sigismund (Holy Roman Emperor, council participant)
Jan Hus (executed 1415, but legacy contested at council)

disconfirming_evidence: The relocation of the Ming capital to Beijing has sometimes been portrayed as an unqualified success, but modern scholarship notes significant costs and disruptions. The move required massive resource transfers from the south, contributed to economic strain, and may have diverted resources from maritime and commercial activities. Some historians argue that the emphasis on Beijing's grandeur and the Forbidden City's construction reflected the Yongle Emperor's personal ambitions rather than broader strategic necessity. The Hussite Wars are sometimes presented as a simple conflict between reform and tradition, but modern scholarship emphasizes the complex social, economic, and political dimensions, including class conflict and regional autonomy issues that transcended purely religious concerns.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1420 is rich for Ming China and Europe, particularly for elite political and military events. The record is dominated by court chronicles, administrative records, and later compilations. African, American, and Asian sources outside the Ming sphere are underrepresented. The perspective is heavily skewed toward official court narratives and elite concerns. Oral traditions, non-elite perspectives, and the experiences of common people are largely absent from surviving records.

events.title: Hussite Wars begin in Bohemia; Jan Žižka emerges as military leader
events.description: Following the execution of Jan Hus in 1415 and the Council of Constance's condemnation of Hussite doctrines, armed conflict erupts in Bohemia between Hussite reformers and Catholic/imperial forces. Jan Žižka, a Bohemian military commander, emerges as the leader of the Hussite forces. The Hussites demand religious reforms including communion in both kinds (sub utraque specie), the removal of church property, and greater autonomy from papal authority. Žižka develops innovative military tactics, including the use of wagon fortifications (tabor) and combined arms strategies, which prove highly effective against larger but less disciplined Catholic armies. The Hussite Wars will continue for approximately 15 years and represent a major religious and political conflict within the Holy Roman Empire.
key_figures: Jan Hus (executed 1415, spiritual inspiration for the movement)
Sigismund of Hungary (Holy Roman Emperor, opponent of the Hussites)

sources.name: Laurence of Březová, Chronicle of the Hussite Wars

These expeditions serve diplomatic, commercial, and intelligence-gathering purposes, establishing Ming tributary relationships and prestige across the Indian Ocean world. The voyages represent the apex of early 15th-century maritime technology and demonstrate Chinese naval superiority.

events.title: Portuguese establish trading post at Ceuta in North Africa
events.description: Portuguese forces, under the direction of Prince Henry the Navigator, capture the North African city of Ceuta from the Marinid dynasty. The conquest represents the first major Portuguese territorial expansion in Africa and establishes a strategic foothold for further exploration and trade along the African coast. Ceuta becomes a base for Portuguese maritime expeditions and commercial operations, facilitating access to trans-Saharan trade routes and establishing a pattern of coastal fortification that will characterize European colonialism. The conquest is celebrated in Portuguese sources as a crusading victory, though it also reflects commercial and strategic interests.

sources.name: Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery of Guinea (Crónica da Descoberta da Guiné), compiled mid-15th century

events.title: Ming Dynasty consolidates control over Yunnan and southwestern China
events.description: The Ming Dynasty, under the Yongle Emperor, consolidates administrative and military control over Yunnan and southwestern China, incorporating these regions more fully into the imperial system. This process involves the integration of local chieftains and ethnic groups into the Ming bureaucratic structure, the establishment of military garrisons, and the extension of Ming legal and administrative systems. The consolidation reflects the Yongle Emperor's broader strategy of centralizing power and expanding Ming territorial control. The process is generally peaceful, involving negotiation and incorporation rather than large-scale military conquest.
key_figures: Local Yunnan chieftains and ethnic leaders

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The consolidation of Ming control over Yunnan occurred during the Yongle period (1402–1424), but the specific date of 1419 is approximate. The process was gradual and involved multiple phases.

disconfirming_evidence: The Hussite Wars are sometimes portrayed as a simple conflict between religious reform and papal authority, but modern scholarship emphasizes the complex social, economic, and political dimensions. The conflict involved class tensions, regional autonomy issues, and resistance to centralized authority that transcended purely religious concerns. Some scholars argue that the Hussite movement was as much a social and political revolution as a religious reform movement. The Portuguese conquest of Ceuta is often celebrated as a crusading victory in Portuguese sources, but modern scholarship emphasizes the commercial and strategic motivations, and notes that the conquest was facilitated by internal divisions within the Marinid dynasty rather than Portuguese military superiority. The significance of the conquest for later Portuguese expansion is sometimes overstated; the immediate impact was limited, and sustained Portuguese exploration of the African coast did not begin until the 1430s.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1419 is moderate for Europe and Ming China, but heavily skewed toward elite political and military events. The record is dominated by court chronicles, administrative records, and later compilations. African, American, and Asian sources outside the Ming sphere and Portuguese sphere are underrepresented. The perspective is heavily skewed toward official court narratives and elite concerns. Oral traditions, non-elite perspectives, and the experiences of common people are largely absent from surviving records. The Hussite Wars are well-documented from the perspective of the Hussite movement and the Catholic opposition, but the experiences of ordinary Bohemian people are less well-recorded.

events.title: Council of Constance concludes; religious reform movement condemned
events.description: The Council of Constance, which had been in session since 1414, concludes its work in 1418. The council had condemned Jan Hus to death in 1415 and had also condemned the teachings of John Wycliffe and other reformers. The council's decisions regarding religious authority, church reform, and the condemnation of heresy remain controversial, particularly in Bohemia, where Hus's followers continue to resist papal authority. The council's failure to address the underlying grievances that motivated reform movements contributes to the escalation of religious conflict in Central Europe, particularly the Hussite Wars which will begin in 1419.
key_figures: Emperor Sigismund (Holy Roman Emperor, council participant)

events.title: Forbidden City construction continues in Beijing
events.description: Construction of the Forbidden City (紫禁城), the imperial palace complex in Beijing, continues under the Yongle Emperor's direction. Work began in 1406 and will be largely finished by 1420. By 1418, the major structural elements are in place, and work is proceeding on interior decoration, furnishings, and landscaping. The complex represents an extraordinary feat of architectural and logistical coordination, employing hundreds of thousands of workers and incorporating advanced timber-frame construction, drainage systems, and decorative arts. The Forbidden City will become the symbolic and administrative center of Ming power.

events.title: Portuguese maritime exploration of African coast accelerates under Henry the Navigator
events.description: Portuguese maritime expeditions, sponsored by Prince Henry the Navigator (Henrique o Navegador), continue systematic exploration of the West African coast. By 1418, Portuguese ships have established a pattern of coastal reconnaissance and are beginning to establish trading posts and fortifications along the Guinea coast. These voyages represent the beginning of European maritime expansion and the Atlantic slave trade, driven by commercial interest in gold, spices, and enslaved people. The expeditions employ improved navigation technology (astrolabe, caravel design) and establish a pattern of coastal fortification and trade monopoly.

disconfirming_evidence: The Council of Constance is sometimes portrayed as a triumph of papal authority and orthodoxy, but modern scholarship emphasizes that the council's decisions did not resolve the underlying religious and social tensions that motivated reform movements. The condemnation of Jan Hus and other reformers actually contributed to the escalation of conflict, particularly in Bohemia. Some scholars argue that the council's failure to address legitimate grievances regarding church corruption and clerical authority weakened the church's position in Central Europe. The Portuguese exploration of the African coast is sometimes presented as a purely commercial and exploratory enterprise, but modern scholarship emphasizes the role of slavery and the exploitation of African peoples from the beginning of Portuguese contact.

events.title: Ming Dynasty consolidates control over maritime trade in the South China Sea
events.description: The Ming Dynasty, under the Yongle Emperor, consolidates control over maritime trade routes in the South China Sea, establishing a system of maritime taxation and regulation. This process involves the suppression of piracy, the establishment of official trading posts, and the regulation of merchant vessels. The consolidation reflects the Yongle Emperor's broader strategy of centralizing power and controlling commerce. The system of maritime regulation will support the massive treasure voyages of Zheng He and will establish Ming dominance over Indian Ocean trade networks.

events.title: Council of Constance continues; John Hus execution aftermath and Hussite Wars preparation
events.description: The Council of Constance (1414–1418) continues its work on church reform and the Western Schism. John Hus was executed in 1415; his death galvanizes Bohemian resistance and sets conditions for the Hussite Wars (1419–1434). In 1416, tensions escalate as Hussite sympathizers organize and the Council debates ecclesiastical authority. This conflict will reshape Central European politics and religious identity for decades.
key_figures: Pope Martin V (elected 1417, but council ongoing)
Sigismund of Luxembourg (Holy Roman Emperor, Council participant)
Jan Žižka (future Hussite military leader, organizing in 1416)

events.certainty_note: Council proceedings are well-documented in contemporary records. The escalation of Hussite organization in 1416 is attested in multiple sources.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure voyages continue; fourth voyage reaches East Africa and Arabian Peninsula
events.region: Indian Ocean, East Africa, Arabian Peninsula, Ming China
events.description: Admiral Zheng He's fourth major voyage (1413–1415, some sources extend to 1416) demonstrates Ming naval supremacy and diplomatic reach. Massive treasure ships carry envoys, gifts, and trade goods to ports across the Indian Ocean, including Aden, Mogadishu, and the East African coast. These voyages project Ming power, gather tribute, and establish tributary relationships. The scale is unprecedented: fleets of 300+ ships with crews exceeding 27,000. By 1416, the voyages are at their peak in terms of geographic reach and political impact.
key_figures: Zheng He (Admiral, Muslim eunuch commanding the fleet)
Yongle Emperor (Ming ruler sponsoring the voyages)
Local rulers in East Africa and Arabia (receiving Ming envoys)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Ming Veritable Records, official court records)
Ma Huan, Yingya Shenglan (Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores, written 1416–1422, participant account)
Fei Xin, Xingcha Shenglan (Triumphant Voyage of the Star Raft, written 1436, participant account)

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's voyages are extensively documented in Ming records. The fourth voyage is dated 1413–1415 in most sources, with some ambiguity about whether it extended into 1416. Assigned here as the voyage's impact was felt through 1416.

events.title: Yongle Emperor consolidates power; Forbidden City construction nears completion
events.description: The Forbidden City, begun in 1406, is substantially completed by 1416 under the Yongle Emperor's direction. This monumental palace complex symbolizes Ming centralization and imperial authority. The construction project involved hundreds of thousands of workers and represents a massive investment in state power. By 1416, the complex is functional, and the emperor has firmly consolidated control after the Jingnan Rebellion (1399–1402). The Yongle reign (1402–1424) marks the height of early Ming power and cultural achievement.
key_figures: Cai Xin (chief eunuch, major influence on court)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Ming Veritable Records)

events.certainty_note: The Forbidden City's construction timeline is well-documented. Completion date is traditionally given as 1420, but substantial portions were functional by 1416.

events.title: Venice and Hungary sign peace treaty; Venetian-Ottoman tensions in Adriatic
events.region: Venice, Hungary, Ottoman Empire, Adriatic Sea
events.description: Venice and Hungary negotiate peace after years of conflict over Dalmatian territories and trade routes. The treaty reflects the shifting balance of power in the Eastern Mediterranean as the Ottoman Empire expands. Venice maintains its commercial dominance but faces increasing pressure from Ottoman naval power. This period marks the beginning of Venice's long decline as a Mediterranean superpower, though it remains wealthy and influential through the 16th century.
key_figures: Venetian Doge (leadership of the Venetian Republic)
Ottoman Sultan Mehmed I (indirect pressure through expansion)

sources.name: Venetian state archives and diplomatic records (contemporary documentation)
Hungarian royal chronicles (contemporary or near-contemporary)

events.certainty_note: The treaty is attested in diplomatic records, though exact dating varies slightly in sources (1416–1417). Assigned to 1416 based on majority of sources.

events.title: Pachacuti consolidates Inca Empire; territorial expansion accelerates
events.description: Pachacuti (Pachacutec Inca Yupanqui), the ninth Inca ruler, continues his aggressive expansion of the Inca Empire. By 1416, he has defeated the Chancas and consolidated control over the central Andes. His reign (traditionally dated 1438–1471, though some scholars place the beginning earlier) marks the transformation of the Inca state from a regional power to a vast empire. The dating of Pachacuti's reign is contested; some sources place his rise to power in the 1430s–1440s, while others suggest earlier dates. By 1416, if the earlier chronology is correct, he would be consolidating power; if the later chronology is correct, he has not yet risen to prominence.

sources.name: Quipucamayoc oral traditions (recorded by Spanish chroniclers in 16th century)
Pedro Cieza de León, Crónica del Perú (written 1553, based on interviews and oral tradition)
Archaeological evidence from Inca sites (stratigraphy, radiocarbon dating)

events.certainty_note: Inca chronology is highly contested. Spanish chroniclers recorded oral traditions decades or centuries after events. Radiocarbon dating and archaeological evidence suggest Pachacuti's rise occurred in the mid-15th century, but exact dates are uncertain. Assigned to 1416 based on traditional chronologies, with significant caveats.

events.title: Portuguese exploration of African coast continues; Prince Henry the Navigator's school active
events.description: Prince Henry the Navigator of Portugal continues to sponsor expeditions along the West African coast. By 1416, Portuguese explorers have reached beyond Cape Bojador (rounded 1434 in traditional accounts, though some sources suggest earlier attempts). Henry's court at Sagres becomes a center for cartography, navigation, and geographic knowledge. These voyages are driven by commercial interests (gold, spices, enslaved people), religious zeal (converting Muslims), and geographic curiosity. The Portuguese exploration program will eventually lead to the rounding of the Cape of Good Hope (1488) and the sea route to India (1498).

Portuguese sea captains and navigators (names often unrecorded for early voyages)

sources.name: Portuguese royal records and chronicles (contemporary or near-contemporary)
Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Crónica da Guiné (Chronicle of Guinea, written 1450s, based on court records)

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration in 1416 is attested in general terms, though specific voyages and dates are often vague in sources. The broader program is confirmed; individual expedition details are less certain.

events.title: Joan of Arc born (traditional date); early life in Domrémy
events.description: Joan of Arc is traditionally dated as born in 1412, though some sources give 1411 or 1413. If 1416 is used, it represents a later estimate. Joan's early life in the village of Domrémy in northeastern France is poorly documented; most accounts come from her trial records (1431) and later hagiographies. By 1416, she would be a child in a region affected by the Hundred Years' War. Her later military leadership (1429–1431) will make her one of the most famous figures of the medieval period.
key_figures: Joan of Arc (Jeanne d'Arc, future military leader)

sources.name: Joan of Arc trial records (Procès de condamnation, 1431, contemporary to her adult life)
Later hagiographies and biographical accounts (16th century onward)

events.certainty_note: Joan's birth date is uncertain; sources vary between 1411 and 1413. The year 1416 is a later estimate and is not well-supported. Included here as a cultural reference point, but with low confidence in the specific dating.

events.title: Ottoman Empire under Mehmed I; consolidation after civil unrest
events.description: Mehmed I (r. 1413–1421) rules the Ottoman Empire during a period of consolidation following the Interregnum (1402–1413). By 1416, he has secured his position against rival claimants and is expanding Ottoman control in the Balkans and Anatolia. The empire faces internal challenges from Anatolian beyliks and external pressure from Venice and Hungary, but Mehmed's reign marks a period of relative stability and growth. The Ottomans are becoming the dominant power in the Eastern Mediterranean and Balkans.

sources.name: Ottoman court records and chronicles (contemporary or near-contemporary)
Ashikpashazade, Tevarih-i Al-i Osman (History of the House of Osman, written 15th century)

events.certainty_note: Mehmed I's reign and consolidation are well-documented in Ottoman sources. The year 1416 falls clearly within his reign.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Pachacuti's rise to power is highly contested. Traditional Inca chronologies place his reign at 1438–1471, but some modern scholars argue for earlier dates (1410s–1420s) based on archaeological evidence and reanalysis of Spanish chronicles. The inclusion of Pachacuti in 1416 reflects this uncertainty. Joan of Arc's birth year is uncertain; 1412 is more commonly cited than 1416. The exact completion date of the Forbidden City is traditionally 1420, though substantial portions were functional earlier. These uncertainties are inherent to the historical record rather than errors in this account.
historiographic_note: The year 1416 is well-documented for Europe and East Asia, with rich archival sources. European sources are abundant but often focus on political and religious elites; economic and social history of common people is less well-recorded. Chinese sources are comprehensive and official, but may reflect court biases. African, American, and Oceanic sources are sparse or absent from European-trained historiography; oral traditions and archaeological evidence are the primary sources for these regions, and they are often underrepresented in Western historical narratives. The Council of Constance records are exceptionally detailed, while Ottoman sources are less abundant but still substantial. The Inca chronology remains contested due to the absence of written records and reliance on oral traditions recorded by Spanish colonizers decades after the fact.

events.title: John Hus executed at the Council of Constance; religious reform and heresy trial
events.description: John Hus, a Bohemian religious reformer and theologian, is tried for heresy at the Council of Constance and executed by burning on July 6, 1415. Hus had challenged papal authority and advocated for church reform, including communion in both kinds for laypeople. Despite a safe-conduct from Emperor Sigismund, he is condemned and executed. His death becomes a rallying point for Bohemian nationalism and religious dissent, directly precipitating the Hussite Wars (1419–1434). Hus's execution represents the Council's commitment to suppressing heresy and maintaining ecclesiastical authority, but it backfires politically by galvanizing opposition in Bohemia.
key_figures: Emperor Sigismund (Holy Roman Emperor, granted safe-conduct)
Pope John XXIII (deposed by council in 1415)

sources.name: Council of Constance records and trial documents (contemporary primary sources)
Ulrich of Richenthal, Chronicle of the Council of Constance (written 1420s, near-contemporary)
Hus's own writings and letters (contemporary primary sources)

events.certainty_note: Hus's execution date is precisely documented. The trial records are extensive and contemporary. The significance and consequences are well-attested.

events.title: Battle of Agincourt; English victory in the Hundred Years' War
events.description: On October 25, 1415, English forces under King Henry V defeat a much larger French army at Agincourt in Normandy. The English victory is achieved through superior tactics (use of longbowmen), discipline, and favorable terrain. The French suffer catastrophic losses, with estimates of 6,000–10,000 killed compared to perhaps 400–1,600 English casualties. Agincourt becomes one of the most famous medieval battles and a symbol of English military prowess. The victory strengthens Henry V's position and leads to the Treaty of Troyes (1420), which recognizes Henry as heir to the French throne. The battle marks a turning point in the Hundred Years' War, though French recovery will eventually lead to English expulsion from France by 1453.
key_figures: Charles VI (King of France, not present at battle)
Duke of Alençon, Duke of Brabant (French commanders, killed)

sources.name: English royal chronicles and records (contemporary or near-contemporary)
Jean Froissart, Chronicles (written earlier, but updated accounts exist)
Gesta Henrici Quinti (Deeds of Henry V, written by a chaplain in Henry's army, contemporary)

events.certainty_note: Agincourt is one of the best-documented medieval battles. The date, location, and general course of the battle are firmly established. Casualty figures vary in sources but are in the ranges cited.

events.title: Portuguese conquest of Ceuta; beginning of Portuguese expansion in North Africa
events.description: Portuguese forces under Prince Henry the Navigator capture the North African city of Ceuta from the Marinid Sultanate on August 21, 1415. The conquest is motivated by religious zeal (crusade against Islam), strategic positioning (control of the Strait of Gibraltar), and commercial interests (access to trade routes and gold). Ceuta becomes the first permanent Portuguese possession in Africa and marks the beginning of Portuguese overseas expansion. The conquest is celebrated as a crusade victory in Portugal but is strategically limited; Ceuta remains isolated and difficult to supply. Nevertheless, it symbolizes Portuguese ambitions and sets the stage for further exploration of the African coast.
key_figures: Prince Henry the Navigator (Portuguese royal, sponsor)
King John I of Portugal (father of Henry, supporter)

sources.name: Portuguese royal chronicles and records (contemporary or near-contemporary)
Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Crónica da Tomada de Ceuta (Chronicle of the Conquest of Ceuta, written 1450s)

events.certainty_note: The conquest of Ceuta is well-documented in Portuguese sources. The date and general circumstances are firmly established.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure voyages; third voyage reaches East Africa
events.region: Indian Ocean, East Africa, Ming China
events.description: Admiral Zheng He's third major voyage (1409–1411, with some sources extending to 1415) demonstrates Ming naval power and diplomatic reach. The massive fleet of treasure ships carries envoys, gifts, and trade goods across the Indian Ocean to East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and the Persian Gulf. The voyages establish tributary relationships and gather exotic goods and animals (giraffes, ostriches, zebras) for the Ming court. By 1415, the voyages are at their peak in terms of scale and geographic reach. The fleet includes over 300 ships with crews exceeding 27,000. These voyages represent the height of Ming maritime power before the program is curtailed in the 1430s.
key_figures: Zheng He (Admiral, Muslim eunuch commanding the fleet)
Yongle Emperor (Ming ruler sponsoring the voyages)
Ma Huan (fleet official, chronicler of the voyages)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Ming Veritable Records, official court records)
Ma Huan, Yingya Shenglan (Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores, written 1416–1422, participant account)

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's voyages are extensively documented in Ming records. The third voyage is dated 1409–1411 in most sources, though some extend it to 1415.

events.title: Council of Constance deposes Pope John XXIII; Western Schism nears resolution
events.description: The Council of Constance deposes Pope John XXIII (Baldassare Cossa) in May 1415, removing one of three rival claimants to the papacy. The Western Schism (1378–1417) has divided the Catholic Church among competing papal claimants. The Council's action is a major step toward resolving the schism, though the process will not be fully completed until 1417 with the election of Pope Martin V. The deposition of John XXIII is controversial; he had been a major patron of the council but is removed when his authority is challenged. The council's assertion of its authority over the pope represents a significant moment in ecclesiastical politics.

sources.name: Council of Constance records and decrees (contemporary documentation)
Ulrich of Richenthal, Chronicle of the Council of Constance (written 1420s)

events.certainty_note: The deposition of John XXIII is precisely documented in council records. The date and circumstances are well-established.
cross_references: 1417-001 (Western Schism resolved with election of Martin V)

events.title: Inca Empire under Viracocha Inca; territorial consolidation in the Andes
events.description: Viracocha Inca (traditional dates 1410–1438) rules the Inca Empire during a period of consolidation and expansion. By 1415, he has secured control over the central Andes and is expanding Inca territory through military campaigns and diplomatic alliances. The Inca state is becoming increasingly centralized and militarized. Viracocha Inca is traditionally succeeded by Pachacuti, though the exact dating and nature of this transition is contested. The Inca chronology is highly uncertain due to the absence of written records and reliance on oral traditions recorded by Spanish colonizers.

sources.name: Quipucamayoc oral traditions (recorded by Spanish chroniclers in 16th century)

events.certainty_note: Inca chronology is highly contested. Spanish chroniclers recorded oral traditions decades or centuries after events. Radiocarbon dating and archaeological evidence suggest Viracocha Inca's reign occurred in the 15th century, but exact dates are uncertain.

events.title: Venice and Genoa continue commercial rivalry; Mediterranean trade competition
events.description: Venice and Genoa, the two dominant maritime republics of the Mediterranean, continue their long-standing commercial and political rivalry. By 1415, Venice has achieved dominance in Mediterranean trade, particularly in spices and luxury goods from the East. Genoa, weakened by internal conflicts and competition from Venice, is declining in power. The rivalry between these two republics shapes Mediterranean politics and economics throughout the 15th century. Venice's dominance will eventually be challenged by the Ottoman Empire and by the opening of Atlantic trade routes.
key_figures: Venetian Doge (leadership of the Venetian Republic)
Genoese Doge (leadership of the Genoese Republic)
Merchant families (Contarini, Morosini, Doria, Spinola, etc.)

events.title: Mehmed I becomes Ottoman Sultan; consolidation of Ottoman power after Interregnum
events.description: Mehmed I (r. 1413–1421) consolidates his position as Ottoman Sultan following the Interregnum (1402–1413). By 1415, he has secured his authority against rival claimants and is expanding Ottoman control in the Balkans and Anatolia. The Ottoman Empire is becoming the dominant power in the Eastern Mediterranean and Balkans. Mehmed I's reign marks a period of relative stability and growth after the chaos of the Interregnum. The Ottomans face challenges from Anatolian beyliks and external pressure from Venice and Hungary, but Mehmed's strong leadership enables consolidation.
key_figures: Murad II (future sultan, son of Mehmed I)

The significance of the Venice-Genoa rivalry in 1415 is less dramatic than in earlier periods; by this time, Venice's dominance is largely established, and Genoa's decline is well underway.
historiographic_note: The year 1415 is exceptionally well-documented for Europe, with rich archival sources for the Council of Constance, the Battle of Agincourt, and the Portuguese conquest of Ceuta. African, American, and Oceanic sources are sparse or absent from European-trained historiography; oral traditions and archaeological evidence are the primary sources for these regions. European sources may overrepresent the significance of European events (Agincourt, Ceuta) relative to events in other regions.

events.title: Council of Constance opens; major ecclesiastical assembly to address Western Schism
events.description: The Council of Constance opens on November 5, 1414, as one of the largest and most important ecclesiastical assemblies of the medieval period. The council is convened to address the Western Schism (1378–1417), which has divided the Catholic Church among competing papal claimants. The council brings together bishops, theologians, princes, and representatives from across Christendom. Major issues include the resolution of the schism, the suppression of heresy (particularly the teachings of John Wycliffe and Jan Hus), and church reform. The council will run until 1418 and will result in the election of Pope Martin V, effectively ending the schism. The council also condemns John Hus to death (1415), an action that will have major political consequences.
key_figures: Pope John XXIII (one of three papal claimants, initially supporter)
John Hus (Bohemian reformer, tried and condemned)

The fleet includes over 300 ships with crews exceeding 27,000. These voyages represent the height of Ming maritime power before the program is curtailed in the 1430s.

events.title: Yongle Emperor consolidates Ming power; Forbidden City construction continues
events.description: The Yongle Emperor (r. 1402–1424) continues to consolidate Ming power and oversee the construction of the Forbidden City, begun in 1406. By 1414, the palace complex is nearing completion and represents a massive investment in state power and imperial authority. The Yongle reign marks the height of early Ming power and cultural achievement. The emperor has firmly consolidated control after the Jingnan Rebellion (1399–1402) and is pursuing an expansionist foreign policy, including the sponsorship of Zheng He's treasure voyages.

events.title: Hundred Years' War continues; English campaigns in France under Henry V
events.region: Kingdom of France, Kingdom of England
events.description: The Hundred Years' War (1337–1453) continues with English forces under King Henry V conducting campaigns in France. By 1414, Henry V is preparing for major military operations that will culminate in the Battle of Agincourt (1415). The war is driven by English claims to the French throne and competition for control of French territories. The conflict has devastated large parts of France and has become a defining feature of both English and French politics. Henry V's aggressive prosecution of the war will lead to major English victories and territorial gains in the early 1420s.

events.title: Mehmed I becomes Ottoman Sultan; consolidation after Interregnum
events.description: Mehmed I (r. 1413–1421) becomes Ottoman Sultan, consolidating power after the Interregnum (1402–1413). The Interregnum was a period of civil conflict following the defeat of Bayezid I by Timur at the Battle of Ankara (1402). Mehmed I's accession marks the end of this period of instability and the beginning of a new phase of Ottoman consolidation and expansion. Mehmed I faces challenges from rival claimants and Anatolian beyliks, but he successfully establishes his authority. His reign marks a period of relative stability and growth, setting the stage for later Ottoman expansion under his successors.

events.title: Inca Empire under Viracocha Inca; territorial expansion in the Andes
events.description: Viracocha Inca (traditional dates 1410–1438) rules the Inca Empire during a period of consolidation and expansion. By 1413, he has secured control over the central Andes and is expanding Inca territory through military campaigns and diplomatic alliances. The Inca state is becoming increasingly centralized and militarized. Viracocha Inca is traditionally succeeded by Pachacuti, though the exact dating and nature of this transition is contested. The Inca chronology is highly uncertain due to the absence of written records and reliance on oral traditions recorded by Spanish colonizers.

sources.name: Quipucamayoc oral traditions (recorded by Spanish chroniclers in 16th century)
Pedro Cieza de León, Crónica del Perú (written 1553, based on interviews and oral tradition)
Archaeological evidence from Inca sites (stratigraphy, radiocarbon dating)

events.certainty_note: Inca chronology is highly contested. Spanish chroniclers recorded oral traditions decades or centuries after events. Radiocarbon dating and archaeological evidence suggest Viracocha Inca's reign occurred in the 15th century, but exact dates are uncertain.

events.title: Venice maintains Mediterranean dominance; commercial and naval power
events.description: Venice continues to dominate Mediterranean trade and maintains its position as the leading maritime power in the region. By 1413, Venice has achieved commercial supremacy through control of trade routes to the East, particularly spices and luxury goods. The Venetian Republic is wealthy and politically influential, though it faces increasing competition from the Ottoman Empire and challenges from other European powers. Venice's dominance will eventually be challenged by the Ottoman conquest of Constantinople (1453) and by the opening of Atlantic trade routes.
key_figures: Venetian Doge (leadership of the Venetian Republic)

sources.name: Venetian state archives and chronicles (contemporary documentation)
Merchant account books and trade records (contemporary documentation)

events.certainty_note: Venetian dominance in Mediterranean trade is well-documented in contemporary sources. The year 1413 falls within a period of Venetian commercial supremacy.
cross_references: 1453-001 (Ottoman conquest of Constantinople affects Venetian trade)

events.title: Portuguese exploration of African coast; Prince Henry the Navigator's patronage
events.description: Prince Henry the Navigator of Portugal continues to sponsor expeditions along the West African coast. By 1413, Portuguese explorers are beginning to venture beyond previously known territories. Henry's court at Sagres becomes a center for cartography, navigation, and geographic knowledge. These voyages are driven by commercial interests (gold, spices, enslaved people), religious zeal (converting Muslims), and geographic curiosity. The Portuguese exploration program will eventually lead to the rounding of the Cape of Good Hope (1488) and the sea route to India (1498).
key_figures: Prince Henry the Navigator (Portuguese royal, sponsor of exploration)
Portuguese sea captains and navigators (names often unrecorded for early voyages)

sources.name: Portuguese royal records and chronicles (contemporary or near-contemporary)
Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Crónica da Guiné (Chronicle of Guinea, written 1450s, based on court records)

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration in 1413 is attested in general terms, though specific voyages and dates are often vague in sources. The broader program is confirmed; individual expedition details are less certain.

events.title: Western Schism in Catholic Church; multiple papal claimants
events.description: The Western Schism (1378–1417) continues to divide the Catholic Church among competing papal claimants. By 1413, there are three rival popes claiming legitimacy: Pope John XXIII (Baldassare Cossa), Pope Gregory XII (Angelo Correr), and an antipope. The schism has weakened papal authority and created confusion among the faithful. The Council of Constance, which will open in 1414, is convened to resolve this crisis. The schism will be formally ended in 1417 with the election of Pope Martin V.
key_figures: Pope John XXIII (Baldassare Cossa, one of three claimants)
Pope Gregory XII (Angelo Correr, another claimant)
Antipope (third claimant, name varies by source)
Emperor Sigismund (Holy Roman Emperor, will convene Council of Constance)

sources.name: Papal records and chronicles (contemporary documentation)
Church council records and decrees (contemporary documentation)

events.certainty_note: The Western Schism is extensively documented in ecclesiastical sources. The year 1413 falls clearly within the period of the schism.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Inca rulers is highly contested. Traditional chronologies place Viracocha Inca at 1410–1438, but modern scholars debate these dates based on archaeological evidence and reanalysis of Spanish chronicles. Zheng He's first voyage is dated 1405–1407 in most sources, though some extend it to 1413; the assignment to 1413 reflects this ambiguity. The significance of Portuguese exploration in 1413 is less dramatic than in later years; the major conquest of Ceuta occurs in 1415, and the rounding of Cape Bojador is traditionally dated to 1434.
historiographic_note: The year 1413 is well-documented for Europe, with rich archival sources for the Western Schism and the Hundred Years' War. Chinese sources are comprehensive and official for Zheng He's voyages. Ottoman sources are less abundant but still substantial. African, American, and Oceanic sources are sparse or absent from European-trained historiography; oral traditions and archaeological evidence are the primary sources for these regions. The Inca chronology remains contested due to the absence of written records and reliance on oral traditions recorded by Spanish colonizers decades after the fact. European sources may overrepresent the significance of European events relative to events in other regions.

graph_edges.note: Mehmed I's accession begins a reign that will last until 1421
Western Schism crisis leads to convening of Council of Constance
English military campaigns in France culminate in Battle of Agincourt

era_context: The early 15th century is marked by the consolidation of Ming Dynasty power under the Yongle Emperor, Zheng He's treasure voyages at their height, the Hundred Years' War between England and France, the Ottoman Empire's consolidation under Mehmed I (who will accede in 1413), and the Western Schism in the Catholic Church. The Inca Empire is expanding under Viracocha Inca (traditional dating disputed). Joan of Arc is traditionally dated as born in 1412 (though sources vary).
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan African kingdoms (limited European documentation)
Pacific Islander societies (no written records)
Interior South American societies beyond Inca sphere

events.title: Joan of Arc born (traditional date); early life in Domrémy
events.description: Joan of Arc is traditionally dated as born in 1412, though some sources give 1411 or 1413. Joan's early life in the village of Domrémy in northeastern France is poorly documented; most accounts come from her trial records (1431) and later hagiographies. By 1412, she would be a child in a region affected by the Hundred Years' War. Her later military leadership (1429–1431) will make her one of the most famous figures of the medieval period. The exact date of her birth is uncertain, and the year 1412 is one of several estimates.
key_figures: Joan of Arc (Jeanne d'Arc, future military leader)

sources.name: Joan of Arc trial records (Procès de condamnation, 1431, contemporary to her adult life)
Later hagiographies and biographical accounts (16th century onward)

events.certainty_note: Joan's birth date is uncertain; sources vary between 1411 and 1413. The year 1412 is a traditional estimate but is not definitively established. Included here as a cultural reference point, but with low confidence in the specific dating.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure voyages continue; massive fleet operations
events.region: Indian Ocean, East Africa, Arabian Peninsula, Ming China
events.description: Admiral Zheng He's treasure voyages continue under the sponsorship of the Yongle Emperor. By 1412, the voyages are at their peak in terms of scale and geographic reach. The massive fleet of treasure ships carries envoys, gifts, and trade goods across the Indian Ocean to East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and the Persian Gulf. The voyages establish tributary relationships and gather exotic goods and animals for the Ming court. The fleet includes over 300 ships with crews exceeding 27,000. These voyages represent the height of Ming maritime power before the program is curtailed in the 1430s.
key_figures: Zheng He (Admiral, Muslim eunuch commanding the fleet)
Yongle Emperor (Ming ruler sponsoring the voyages)
Ma Huan (fleet official, chronicler of the voyages)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Ming Veritable Records, official court records)
Ma Huan, Yingya Shenglan (Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores, written 1416–1422, participant account)

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's voyages are extensively documented in Ming records. The year 1412 falls within the period of active voyages.

events.title: Yongle Emperor consolidates Ming power; Forbidden City construction continues
events.description: The Yongle Emperor (r. 1402–1424) continues to consolidate Ming power and oversee the construction of the Forbidden City, begun in 1406. By 1412, the palace complex is under active construction and represents a massive investment in state power and imperial authority. The Yongle reign marks the height of early Ming power and cultural achievement. The emperor has firmly consolidated control after the Jingnan Rebellion (1399–1402) and is pursuing an expansionist foreign policy, including the sponsorship of Zheng He's treasure voyages.
key_figures: Cai Xin (chief eunuch, major influence on court)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Ming Veritable Records)
Archaeological evidence from the Forbidden City (excavation and architectural analysis)

events.certainty_note: The Forbidden City's construction timeline is well-documented. By 1412, the complex is under active construction.

events.title: Hundred Years' War continues; English campaigns in France under Henry V
events.region: Kingdom of France, Kingdom of England
events.description: The Hundred Years' War (1337–1453) continues with English forces under King Henry V conducting campaigns in France. By 1412, Henry V has recently become king (1413) and is consolidating his power in England. The war is driven by English claims to the French throne and competition for control of French territories. The conflict has devastated large parts of France and has become a defining feature of both English and French politics. Henry V's aggressive prosecution of the war will lead to major English victories and territorial gains in the early 1420s.
key_figures: Henry V (King of England, military commander, accedes 1413)
Henry IV (King of England, father of Henry V, dies 1413)
Charles VI (King of France, mentally unstable)
Duke of Burgundy (powerful French noble, sometimes allied with England)

sources.name: English royal chronicles and records (contemporary or near-contemporary)
French royal chronicles and records (contemporary or near-contemporary)

events.certainty_note: The Hundred Years' War is extensively documented in both English and French sources. The year 1412 falls within a period of English military campaigns.

era_context: The early 15th century saw the Ming Dynasty consolidating power in China under the Yongle Emperor, the Ottoman Empire expanding in the Balkans and Mediterranean, the Hussite Wars raging in Bohemia following Jan Hus's execution, and European Renaissance humanism beginning to flourish in Italy. The Age of Exploration was imminent; the Zheng He treasure voyages were underway. In the Americas, the Aztec Empire was at its height under Montezuma I, and the Inca Empire was expanding under Pachacuti.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; oral traditions not well-documented in European sources)
Southeast Asia (fragmentary records; Vietnamese and Thai sources underrepresented in Western historiography)
Pacific Islands (no written records for this period)

events.title: Zheng He's Third Treasure Voyage departs from China
events.region: Ming China, Indian Ocean, East Africa
events.description: Admiral Zheng He, commanding a massive fleet of over 100 vessels and approximately 27,000 personnel, departed Nanjing on his third major voyage of exploration. The expedition sailed to Southeast Asia, India, the Arabian Peninsula, and East Africa, establishing tributary relationships and demonstrating Ming naval supremacy. This voyage lasted until 1412 and represented the apex of Chinese maritime exploration before the Treasure Voyages were curtailed in the 1430s.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Zheng He's voyage inscriptions and steles
Ma Huan, Ying-yai Sheng-lan (Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores)

events.certainty_note: Departure date well-documented in Ming records; voyage dates are established within ±1 year.

events.title: Battle of Harlaw (or Battle of Red Harlaw) in Scotland
events.description: A significant conflict between the Highland forces of Donald of Isles (Lord of the Isles) and the Lowland forces of the Earl of Mar, fought near Harlaw in Aberdeenshire. The battle resulted in a tactical victory for the Lowlanders but demonstrated the military power of the Highlands. The engagement was part of broader struggles over Scottish territorial control and the integration of the Isles into the Scottish kingdom.
key_figures: Alexander Stewart, Earl of Mar (Lowland commander)

sources.name: Andrew of Wyntoun, Orygynale Cronykil of Scotland
Walter Bower, Scotichronicon

events.certainty_note: Date of 1411 is traditional; some sources place it in 1411, others suggest 1412. Contemporary documentation is limited; accounts come from later 15th-century chronicles.

events.title: Council of Constance continues (Session phase)
events.description: The Council of Constance (1414–1418) was in its early sessions in 1411, though the council formally opened in November 1414. However, preparatory gatherings and early ecclesiastical assemblies were occurring in 1411 as the Church prepared to address the Great Schism and heresy. The council would eventually condemn Jan Hus and resolve the papal schism.
key_figures: Pope John XXIII (one of three claimants)

sources.name: Council of Constance records and decrees

events.certainty_note: The formal Council of Constance began in 1414, not 1411. This entry reflects preparatory ecclesiastical activity; dating is approximate.

events.title: Hussite Wars intensify in Bohemia
events.description: Following Jan Hus's execution in 1415, the Hussite Wars (1419–1434) had not yet begun, but religious and social tensions in Bohemia were escalating in 1411. The Hussite movement, demanding church reform and Czech national identity, was gathering momentum. By 1411, radical reformist preachers were active, and the Bohemian nobility was increasingly divided over religious and political authority.

sources.name: Laurence of Březová, Chronicle of the Hussites
Contemporary papal and imperial correspondence

events.certainty_note: The escalation of tensions in 1411 is inferred from broader historical context; specific events for this year are not well-documented.

events.title: Montezuma I consolidates Aztec power
events.description: Montezuma I (Montezuma Ilhuicamina), who ruled 1440–1469, was not yet emperor in 1411. However, the Aztec Empire under Itzcoatl (r. 1427–1440) was in the process of consolidating its dominance over the Valley of Mexico. By 1411, the Mexica were emerging as a major power following their alliance with Texcoco and Tlacopan. The empire was expanding militarily and administratively.

sources.name: Codex Mendoza
Sahagún, Florentine Codex

events.certainty_note: Specific events for 1411 in Aztec history are not well-documented in surviving sources. This entry reflects the broader context of Aztec expansion during this period.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Battle of Harlaw is disputed; some modern scholarship places it in 1411, while other sources suggest 1412 or even earlier. The significance of the battle has been debated—some historians view it as a major turning point in Scottish Highland-Lowland relations, while others see it as a relatively minor skirmish in a longer process of integration. The entry on Bohemian tensions in 1411 is speculative; no major events are specifically documented for this year, and the escalation of Hussite sentiment is inferred from broader trends rather than documented incidents.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1411 is uneven. European events (Scotland, the Holy Roman Empire) are recorded in chronicles, though often with dating uncertainties and retrospective interpretation. Chinese records are rich and contemporary, particularly for the Zheng He voyages. Mesoamerican history relies on post-conquest codices and oral traditions recorded by Spanish friars, introducing significant interpretive challenges. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Pacific are largely absent from surviving written records for this year. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history is underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: Third voyage in the series of Zheng He's treasure voyages
Both represent regional conflicts during a period of religious and political upheaval in Europe
Escalating tensions in Bohemia led to Jan Hus's trial and execution

era_context: The early 15th century continued with the Ming Dynasty under Yongle Emperor at its height, the Ottoman Empire consolidating Balkan territories, the Hussite movement building momentum in Bohemia, and the European Renaissance beginning to flourish. The Zheng He treasure voyages were in their early phases. In the Americas, the Aztec Empire was consolidating power, and the Inca Empire was expanding under Pachacuti.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records)
Southeast Asia (fragmentary records)
Pacific Islands (no written records)
Central Asia (limited documentation)

events.title: Battle of Grunwald (Tannenberg) aftermath and Polish-Lithuanian consolidation
events.description: The Battle of Grunwald (1410) was a decisive victory of the Polish-Lithuanian alliance over the Teutonic Knights, fought in July 1410. By 1410, the aftermath was being consolidated: the First Peace of Thorn (February 1411) would follow, but in 1410 itself, the political and military consequences were being negotiated. The victory marked a turning point in Eastern European power dynamics, diminishing Teutonic Knight dominance and strengthening Polish-Lithuanian union.

sources.name: Jan Długosz, Historia Poloniae
Teutonic Order records
Contemporary Polish and Lithuanian chronicles

events.certainty_note: The battle occurred in July 1410; the year is well-established in multiple contemporary sources.

events.title: Zheng He's Second Treasure Voyage returns to China
events.description: Admiral Zheng He's second major voyage of exploration, which had departed in 1407, returned to China in 1410. The expedition had sailed to Southeast Asia, India, and the Arabian Peninsula, establishing tributary relationships and demonstrating Ming naval power. The return marked the completion of a major diplomatic and exploratory mission.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Ma Huan, Ying-yai Sheng-lan

events.certainty_note: Return date is well-documented in Ming records; dating is secure within ±1 year.

events.title: Council of Pisa aftermath and papal schism continuation
events.description: The Council of Pisa (1409) had attempted to resolve the Great Schism by deposing both papal claimants and electing a new pope (Alexander V). However, by 1410, the schism remained unresolved, with three papal claimants still active: Gregory XII (Rome), Benedict XIII (Avignon), and Alexander V (Pisa). The ecclesiastical crisis continued to destabilize Western Christendom.

sources.name: Council of Pisa records and decrees
Papal correspondence and bulls

events.certainty_note: The continuation of the schism in 1410 is well-documented in papal and conciliar records.

events.title: Henry V becomes King of England
events.description: Henry V ascended to the English throne on March 20, 1413, following the death of his father Henry IV. However, in 1410, Henry (then Prince of Wales) was already a significant political figure, involved in military campaigns in Wales and beginning to assert influence over English policy. His accession was imminent, and his preparations for the Hundred Years' War were underway.
key_figures: Henry V (Prince of Wales, future King)

sources.name: Chronicles of the period (Froissart, Monstrelet)
English royal records and correspondence

events.certainty_note: Henry V did not become king until 1413; this entry reflects his rising prominence in 1410, not a coronation event.

events.title: Inca Empire expansion under Pachacuti
events.description: Pachacuti (Pachacutec Inca Yupanqui), who ruled approximately 1438–1471, was not yet emperor in 1410. However, the Inca Empire under Viracocha Inca was in a period of expansion and consolidation. By 1410, the Inca had established control over much of the central Andes and were beginning the military campaigns that would lead to the empire's greatest territorial extent under Pachacuti.

sources.name: Quipucamayoc (Inca record keepers) oral traditions, recorded by Spanish chroniclers
Garcilaso de la Vega, Royal Commentaries of the Incas

events.certainty_note: Inca chronology is uncertain; dates are approximate and based on later Spanish colonial records and oral traditions. Specific events for 1410 are not documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Henry V's prominence in 1410 is somewhat speculative; while he was certainly active in Welsh campaigns and court politics, the specific significance of 1410 versus adjacent years is not clearly distinguished in sources. The entry on the papal schism reflects continuation rather than a specific event in 1410; no major development occurred in 1410 that distinguished it from 1409 or 1411. Inca chronology is highly uncertain; the assignment of Viracocha Inca's reign to ~1410–1438 is based on later Spanish colonial sources and oral traditions, and modern scholarship debates these dates significantly.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1410 is moderate but uneven. European events (Poland-Lithuania, England, the papacy) are well-recorded in contemporary chronicles and official records. Chinese records are rich and contemporary, particularly for the Zheng He voyages. Mesoamerican and South American history relies on post-conquest sources, introducing significant interpretive challenges and chronological uncertainty. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history is underrepresented. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Pacific are largely absent from surviving written records.

The Zheng He treasure voyages were in their early phases. In the Americas, the Aztec Empire was consolidating power, and the Inca Empire was expanding.

events.title: Council of Pisa convenes to resolve the Great Schism
events.description: The Council of Pisa (1409–1410) convened in March 1409 to address the Great Schism, which had divided the Western Church since 1378. The council attempted to resolve the crisis by deposing both papal claimants (Gregory XII in Rome and Benedict XIII in Avignon) and electing a new pope, Alexander V. However, the council's actions were not universally accepted, and the schism effectively continued with three papal claimants. The council represented a significant attempt at ecclesiastical reform and conciliar authority.
key_figures: Cardinal Baldassare Cossa (later Pope John XXIII)

sources.name: Council of Pisa records and decrees
Papal bulls and correspondence
Contemporary chronicles (Monstrelet, Froissart)

events.certainty_note: The Council of Pisa convened in March 1409; the date is well-established in multiple contemporary sources.

events.title: Zheng He's Second Treasure Voyage departs from China
events.description: Admiral Zheng He, commanding a massive fleet, departed on his second major voyage of exploration in 1409 (some sources date this to 1407). The expedition sailed to Southeast Asia, India, the Arabian Peninsula, and East Africa, establishing tributary relationships and demonstrating Ming naval supremacy. The voyage lasted approximately three years and returned in 1410.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Ma Huan, Ying-yai Sheng-lan

events.certainty_note: The departure date of the second voyage is disputed; some sources place it in 1407, others in 1409. Dating is uncertain by ±2 years.

events.title: Jan Hus becomes rector of Charles University in Prague
events.description: Jan Hus, the Bohemian religious reformer, became rector of Charles University in Prague in 1409. This position gave him significant influence over the university and the Hussite movement, which was gaining momentum in Bohemia. Hus's teachings emphasized church reform, Czech national identity, and the authority of scripture, challenging papal authority and ecclesiastical corruption. His prominence at the university helped spread reformist ideas throughout Bohemia.
key_figures: Jan Hus (Rector of Charles University, reformer)

sources.name: Contemporary Bohemian chronicles
University of Prague records
Laurence of Březová, Chronicle of the Hussites

events.certainty_note: Hus's appointment as rector in 1409 is well-documented in university records and contemporary chronicles.

events.title: Ottoman Empire consolidates Balkan territories
events.description: In 1409, the Ottoman Empire under Mehmed I (r. 1413–1421) was not yet in power; the empire was under Musa II (r. 1421–1422) or in a period of transition. However, the Ottoman Empire was consolidating its control over the Balkans during this period, expanding its territorial holdings and establishing administrative structures. The empire was in a phase of consolidation and preparation for further expansion.

sources.name: Ottoman chronicles and records
Byzantine and Venetian sources

events.certainty_note: Ottoman succession and specific events in 1409 are not clearly documented; this entry reflects the broader context of Ottoman expansion during this period.

events.title: Aztec Empire under Itzcoatl consolidates power
events.description: The Aztec Empire under Itzcoatl (r. 1427–1440) was not yet in power in 1409; the empire was under Chimalpopoca (r. 1417–1427). However, by 1409, the Mexica were emerging as a major power in the Valley of Mexico. The empire was expanding militarily and establishing tributary relationships with neighboring city-states.

sources.name: Codex Mendoza
Sahagún, Florentine Codex

events.certainty_note: Specific events for 1409 in Aztec history are not well-documented in surviving sources. This entry reflects the broader context of Aztec expansion during this period.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Zheng He's second voyage is disputed; some sources place the departure in 1407, others in 1409. The significance of 1409 as a departure date versus 1407 is not definitively established in modern scholarship. The entry on Ottoman consolidation is speculative; no major events are specifically documented for 1409 in Ottoman history. The entry on Aztec expansion reflects broader trends rather than documented incidents for this specific year. Jan Hus's appointment as rector in 1409 is confirmed, but the significance of this event in relation to the broader Hussite movement is sometimes overstated; the movement was already gaining momentum before his appointment.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1409 is moderate but uneven. European events (the Council of Pisa, Jan Hus's appointment) are well-recorded in contemporary sources. Chinese records are rich and contemporary, though the dating of Zheng He's voyages is disputed. Mesoamerican and South American history relies on post-conquest sources, introducing significant interpretive challenges and chronological uncertainty. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward elite political and religious events; economic, social, and cultural history is underrepresented. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Pacific are largely absent from surviving written records. The Council of Pisa is well-documented, but its failure to resolve the schism is sometimes presented as a more decisive failure than it was; the council did achieve some consolidation of ecclesiastical authority, even if the schism formally continued.

The Zheng He treasure voyages were in their early phases. In the Americas, the Aztec Empire was consolidating power, and the Inca Empire was expanding.
