events.title: Ming Dynasty consolidates administrative control
events.description: The Ming Dynasty under the Yongle Emperor (r. 1402–1424) was consolidating administrative control over China in 1408. The emperor had moved the capital from Nanjing to Beijing in 1421 (construction began earlier), and the empire was establishing new administrative structures and military organizations. The Yongle Emperor was at the height of his power, having defeated the Jianwen Emperor and consolidated his authority.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Contemporary Ming administrative records

events.certainty_note: The Yongle Emperor's consolidation of power is well-documented in Ming records; the year 1408 falls within his reign and represents a period of administrative development.

events.title: Zheng He's First Treasure Voyage returns to China
events.description: Admiral Zheng He's first major voyage of exploration, which had departed in 1405, returned to China in 1407 or 1408. The expedition had sailed to Southeast Asia, India, and the Arabian Peninsula, establishing tributary relationships and demonstrating Ming naval supremacy. The return marked the completion of a major diplomatic and exploratory mission that would be followed by subsequent voyages.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Ma Huan, Ying-yai Sheng-lan

events.certainty_note: Return date is documented in Ming records but with some uncertainty; sources place the return in 1407 or 1408, ±1 year.

events.title: Hussite movement gains momentum in Bohemia
events.description: The Hussite movement, inspired by the teachings of Jan Hus and other reformers, was gaining momentum in Bohemia in 1408. Religious and social tensions were escalating as reformist preachers challenged papal authority and ecclesiastical corruption. The movement was building support among the Bohemian nobility, clergy, and common people, setting the stage for the Hussite Wars that would begin in 1419.

sources.name: Contemporary Bohemian chronicles
Papal correspondence and bulls

events.certainty_note: The escalation of Hussite sentiment in 1408 is inferred from broader historical context; specific events for this year are not well-documented.
cross_references: 1409-003 (Jan Hus becomes rector of Charles University)

events.title: Venice and the Ottoman Empire negotiate maritime boundaries
events.description: In 1408, Venice and the Ottoman Empire were engaged in ongoing negotiations over maritime boundaries and trade rights in the Eastern Mediterranean. The two powers had competing interests in the region, and periodic conflicts and treaties characterized their relationship. By 1408, Venice was beginning to lose its dominance in the Eastern Mediterranean as Ottoman naval power grew.

sources.name: Venetian state records and diplomatic correspondence
Ottoman chronicles

events.certainty_note: Venice-Ottoman relations in 1408 are not specifically documented; this entry reflects the broader context of their ongoing competition.

events.title: Inca Empire expansion under Viracocha Inca
events.description: The Inca Empire under Viracocha Inca (r. ~1410–1438) was in a period of expansion and consolidation in 1408. The Inca had established control over much of the central Andes and were beginning military campaigns that would lead to the empire's greatest territorial extent under Pachacuti. Specific events for 1408 are not documented, but the empire was consolidating its power.

sources.name: Quipucamayoc (Inca record keepers) oral traditions, recorded by Spanish chroniclers
Garcilaso de la Vega, Royal Commentaries of the Incas

events.certainty_note: Inca chronology is uncertain; dates are approximate and based on later Spanish colonial records and oral traditions. Specific events for 1408 are not documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The return date of Zheng He's first voyage is disputed; sources place it in 1407 or 1408, with some uncertainty. The significance of 1408 versus 1407 is not clearly distinguished in sources. The entry on Hussite momentum in 1408 is speculative; no major events are specifically documented for this year. The entry on Venice-Ottoman negotiations reflects ongoing relations rather than a specific event in 1408. Inca chronology is highly uncertain; the assignment of Viracocha Inca's reign to ~1410–1438 is based on later Spanish colonial sources and oral traditions, and modern scholarship debates these dates significantly.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1408 is moderate but uneven. Chinese records are rich and contemporary, particularly for the Zheng He voyages, though the exact dating of voyage returns is sometimes uncertain. European events (Bohemian religious tensions, Venice-Ottoman relations) are less clearly documented for this specific year. Mesoamerican and South American history relies on post-conquest sources, introducing significant interpretive challenges and chronological uncertainty. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history is underrepresented. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Pacific are largely absent from surviving written records.

events.title: Zheng He's Second Treasure Voyage departs from China
events.description: Admiral Zheng He, commanding a massive fleet of over 100 vessels and approximately 27,000 personnel, departed on his second major voyage of exploration in 1407 (some sources date this to 1409). The expedition sailed to Southeast Asia, India, the Arabian Peninsula, and East Africa, establishing tributary relationships and demonstrating Ming naval supremacy. The voyage lasted approximately three years and returned in 1410.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Ma Huan, Ying-yai Sheng-lan (Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores)

events.certainty_note: The departure date of the second voyage is disputed; some sources place it in 1407, others in 1409. Dating is uncertain by ±2 years.

events.title: Council of Constance preparations and ecclesiastical tensions
events.description: In 1407, preparations were underway for the Council of Constance (1414–1418), which would address the Great Schism and heresy in the Western Church. Ecclesiastical tensions were escalating as the Council of Pisa (1409) approached. The Church was divided among three papal claimants, and pressure was mounting for a general council to resolve the crisis.
key_figures: Sigismund, Holy Roman Emperor (future convener of Council of Constance)

sources.name: Papal correspondence and bulls
Imperial records

events.certainty_note: Preparations for the Council of Constance were underway in 1407, but no specific events are documented for this year. The council formally opened in 1414.

events.title: Jan Hus's reformist preaching gains influence in Prague
events.description: Jan Hus, the Bohemian religious reformer, was gaining influence through his preaching at the Bethlehem Chapel in Prague in 1407. His teachings emphasized church reform, Czech national identity, and the authority of scripture, challenging papal authority and ecclesiastical corruption. Hus's influence was growing among the Bohemian nobility, clergy, and common people, setting the stage for his appointment as rector of Charles University in 1409.
key_figures: Jan Hus (Reformer, preacher at Bethlehem Chapel)

sources.name: Contemporary Bohemian chronicles
Laurence of Březová, Chronicle of the Hussites

events.certainty_note: Hus's growing influence in 1407 is inferred from broader historical context; specific events for this year are not well-documented.
cross_references: 1409-003 (Jan Hus becomes rector of Charles University)

events.title: Ottoman Empire consolidates Balkan territories
events.description: The Ottoman Empire was consolidating its control over the Balkans in 1407, expanding its territorial holdings and establishing administrative structures. The empire was in a phase of consolidation and preparation for further expansion. Specific events for 1407 are not well-documented, but the empire was strengthening its position in the region.

sources.name: Ottoman chronicles and records
Byzantine and Venetian sources

events.certainty_note: Ottoman consolidation in 1407 is not specifically documented; this entry reflects the broader context of Ottoman expansion during this period.

events.title: Aztec Empire consolidates power in the Valley of Mexico
events.description: The Aztec Empire was consolidating its power in the Valley of Mexico in 1407. The empire was expanding militarily and establishing tributary relationships with neighboring city-states. Specific events for 1407 are not well-documented in surviving sources, but the empire was strengthening its position in the region.

sources.name: Codex Mendoza
Sahagún, Florentine Codex

events.certainty_note: Specific events for 1407 in Aztec history are not well-documented in surviving sources. This entry reflects the broader context of Aztec expansion during this period.

The entries on ecclesiastical preparations, Hussite influence, Ottoman consolidation, and Aztec expansion are all speculative; no major events are specifically documented for 1407 in these regions. These entries reflect broader trends rather than documented incidents for this specific year.

era_context: Early Ming Dynasty (1368–1644) in China under the Yongle Emperor; the Zheng He treasure voyages are in their early planning or initial stages. In Europe, the Renaissance is emerging in Italy; the Council of Constance (1414–1418) is being prepared to address the Western Schism in the Catholic Church. The Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia and the Balkans. The Timurid Renaissance flourishes in Central Asia and Persia under Shah Rukh.

events.title: Death of Tamerlane's son Miran Shah; succession crisis in Timurid Empire
events.description: Miran Shah, son of Timur (Tamerlane) and governor of western Timurid territories, died in 1406, intensifying succession disputes within the Timurid dynasty. His death removed a major power broker and contributed to the fragmentation that would eventually lead to Shah Rukh's consolidation of power. The succession crisis affected trade routes and political stability across Central Asia and Persia.

sources.name: Zafarnama (Book of Victories) by Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi
Historiographic consensus on Timurid succession

events.certainty_note: Date of death is attested in later Timurid chronicles but not in contemporary primary sources. Scholarly consensus places it in 1406, though some sources vary by ±1–2 years.

events.title: Zheng He's early naval preparations; Ming treasure fleet planning
events.description: The Yongle Emperor commissioned preparations for the first of Zheng He's treasure voyages, which would launch in 1407. In 1406, shipbuilding, recruitment, and logistical planning were underway in Nanjing and other Ming ports. This represented an unprecedented commitment to maritime exploration and diplomatic projection of Ming power across the Indian Ocean.
key_figures: Zheng He (Muslim eunuch admiral, commanding officer)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)
The Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores (Ying-yai Sheng-lan) by Ma Huan

events.certainty_note: The first voyage departed in 1407; 1406 is the conventional date for final preparations based on Ming administrative records, though exact timing of planning phases is not precisely documented.

events.title: Donatello completes bronze David; early Renaissance sculpture
events.description: Donatello (Donato di Niccolò Bardi) completed his bronze David, the first free-standing nude male sculpture since antiquity. This work marked a watershed moment in Renaissance art, demonstrating mastery of classical anatomy and humanist ideals. The sculpture was commissioned for the Medici family and became an icon of Florentine civic identity and artistic innovation.

sources.name: Vasari's Lives of the Artists (Vite de' più eccellenti pittori, scultori, e architettori)
Archival records of the Medici family and Florence

events.certainty_note: Completion date is traditionally given as 1406, though some art historians place it as late as 1408–1409. Vasari's account, written in the 16th century, is the primary narrative source.

events.title: Battle of Harran; Ottoman-Timurid conflict
events.description: Ottoman forces under Mehmed I engaged Timurid and allied forces near Harran in a contested engagement over control of Upper Mesopotamian territories. The battle reflected broader Ottoman expansion into regions previously dominated by Timurid influence. Control of Harran was strategically important for trade routes and regional hegemony.

sources.name: Ottoman chronicles (Neşri, Ashikpashazade)
Historiographic consensus on Ottoman-Timurid conflicts

events.certainty_note: Dating of this engagement is uncertain; some sources place it in 1405 or 1407. Ottoman chronicles are not contemporary and often conflate or misdate military engagements. The battle's historical significance is debated among scholars.

events.title: Zheng He appointed as admiral of the treasure fleet
events.description: Zheng He, a Muslim eunuch of Mongol descent, was formally appointed by the Yongle Emperor to command the imperial treasure fleet (baochuan). This appointment elevated Zheng He to one of the most powerful positions in the Ming government and positioned him to lead the first of seven major voyages across the Indian Ocean. His appointment reflected the Yongle Emperor's confidence in eunuch administrators and his ambitions for maritime expansion.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)
Biography of Zheng He in Ming historical records

events.certainty_note: The appointment is recorded in Ming administrative records, though the exact date within 1406 is not specified. Zheng He's biography in the Ming Shilu provides the primary documentation.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Donatello's bronze David as 1406 is contested; some art historians argue for completion dates as late as 1408–1409 based on stylistic analysis and archival gaps. The Battle of Harran's significance and exact date are disputed; some Ottoman historians question whether a major engagement occurred in 1406 or whether earlier/later conflicts have been conflated. Zheng He's appointment date is inferred from voyage departure dates rather than explicitly documented in surviving records.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1406 is uneven. Ming administrative records (Ming Shilu) are relatively rich and contemporary, providing good coverage of Chinese political and maritime events. Italian Renaissance sources are abundant but often written retrospectively (Vasari's Lives, written in the 16th century, is the primary narrative source for Donatello). Ottoman and Timurid sources are later compilations, often written decades or centuries after events, making precise dating difficult. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Northern Europe are essentially undocumented for this year in surviving written sources. The historical record is heavily skewed toward literate, centralized states (Ming China, Ottoman Empire, Italian city-states) and away from oral cultures and decentralized societies.

The Ottoman Empire is consolidating control in Anatolia and the Balkans. The Italian Renaissance is accelerating; the Council of Constance is being convened to address the Western Schism.

events.title: Death of Timur (Tamerlane); succession crisis in Timurid Empire
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane), the great Central Asian conqueror and founder of the Timurid Empire, died on February 18, 1405, at Samarkand at approximately 68 years of age. His death triggered an immediate succession crisis among his sons and grandsons, fragmenting the vast empire he had built. The succession struggle would dominate Central Asian and Persian politics for the next decade, eventually resulting in Shah Rukh's consolidation of power. Timur's death marked the end of an era of unified Timurid expansion and the beginning of a period of internal conflict.

sources.name: Zafarnama (Book of Victories) by Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi
Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) references in Ming sources
Historiographic consensus on Timurid succession

events.certainty_note: Timur's death date is well-attested in multiple Timurid and Islamic sources. The date February 18, 1405, is the standard scholarly consensus, though some sources give slightly different dates within early 1405.
cross_references: 1406-001 (Miran Shah's death; continuation of succession crisis)

events.title: Forbidden City construction begins; Beijing becomes Ming capital
events.description: The Yongle Emperor commissioned the construction of the Forbidden City (Zijin Cheng) in Beijing, marking the formal shift of the Ming capital from Nanjing to Beijing. Construction began in 1406 (some sources say 1405) and would continue until 1420. The Forbidden City represented an unprecedented architectural and logistical undertaking, requiring the mobilization of hundreds of thousands of workers, artisans, and materials. The move to Beijing reflected the Yongle Emperor's strategic focus on defending against Mongol threats from the north and projecting Ming power into Central Asia.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)
Archaeological and architectural records of the Forbidden City

events.certainty_note: Construction dates are traditionally given as 1406–1420, though some sources place the start in 1405. Ming administrative records provide the primary documentation, though exact commencement dates are not always precisely specified.

events.title: Council of Constance convened; Western Schism begins resolution
events.description: The Council of Constance was convened by the Holy Roman Emperor Sigismund and Pope John XXIII to address the Western Schism, in which multiple popes claimed legitimacy. The council (1414–1418) would become one of the most important ecclesiastical assemblies of the medieval period, resulting in the deposition of antipopes and the election of Pope Martin V. The council also condemned Jan Hus and addressed broader questions of papal authority and church reform. The convocation in 1405 represented the beginning of preparations for this transformative council.
key_figures: Pope John XXIII (one of the contending popes)

sources.name: Council of Constance records and decrees
Historiographic consensus on the Western Schism

events.certainty_note: The council formally convened in 1414, not 1405. However, preparations and preliminary negotiations began in 1405. The event is listed here as marking the beginning of the process that would culminate in the council's convocation.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure fleet preparations accelerate
events.description: Following the Yongle Emperor's decision to launch maritime expeditions, preparations for Zheng He's treasure fleet intensified in 1405. Shipbuilding, recruitment of crews, and logistical planning were underway in Nanjing and other Ming ports. The fleet would depart in 1407, making 1405–1406 crucial years for assembly and preparation.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: Preparations are inferred from the 1407 departure date and Ming administrative records. Specific events in 1405 are not individually documented.

events.title: Mehmed I becomes Ottoman Sultan; consolidation of Ottoman power
events.description: Mehmed I (Mehmed Çelebi), son of Murad II, became Ottoman Sultan in 1405, consolidating Ottoman control over Anatolia and the Balkans. His reign marked a period of Ottoman expansion and administrative consolidation. Mehmed I faced internal challenges from rival claimants and external threats from Venice, Hungary, and the Byzantine Empire, but successfully maintained Ottoman territorial integrity and expanded Ottoman influence in the eastern Mediterranean.

sources.name: Ottoman chronicles (Neşri, Ashikpashazade)
Historiographic consensus on Ottoman succession

events.certainty_note: Mehmed I's accession date is traditionally given as 1405, though some sources vary by ±1 year. Ottoman chronicles are not contemporary and often conflate succession dates.

disconfirming_evidence: The Council of Constance is often dated to 1414–1418, not 1405. While preparations began earlier, the council itself did not convene until 1414. Mehmed I's accession date is disputed; some Ottoman sources place it in 1404 or 1406. The exact commencement date of Forbidden City construction is debated; some sources say 1406, others 1405. Timur's death date is well-attested, but the immediate succession crisis and its resolution are described differently in various Timurid and Islamic sources, with some accounts emphasizing different claimants' legitimacy.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1405 is moderate but uneven. Ming administrative records (Ming Shilu) provide good coverage of Chinese political and maritime events. Ottoman sources are later compilations, often written decades after events, making precise dating difficult. Timurid sources (Zafarnama) are also later compilations, written in the 16th century, though they draw on earlier accounts. European sources for the Council of Constance are abundant but mostly post-1414. The historical record is heavily skewed toward literate, centralized states and away from oral cultures and decentralized societies. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Northern Europe are essentially undocumented for this year.

The Ottoman Empire is expanding in Anatolia and the Balkans. The Italian Renaissance is accelerating; humanist scholarship is flourishing in Florence and other Italian city-states.

events.title: Timur's final military campaign; preparations for Central Asian expansion
events.region: Samarkand and Central Asia, Timurid Empire
events.description: In his final year of life, Timur (Tamerlane) was planning a massive military campaign against the Ming Dynasty in China, reportedly motivated by the Yongle Emperor's refusal to pay tribute. Timur assembled a vast army at Samarkand and made preparations for the campaign, though he died in February 1405 before the expedition could depart. This final campaign plan reflected Timur's continued ambitions for conquest despite his advanced age (approximately 67–68 years old).
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (Ming ruler, target of planned campaign)

sources.name: Zafarnama (Book of Victories) by Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi
Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: The campaign plan is attested in Timurid sources (Zafarnama), but the Zafarnama was written in the 16th century and may conflate or embellish events. Ming sources do not mention the planned campaign, suggesting it may have been less significant than Timurid sources suggest.

events.title: Yongle Emperor consolidates power; purges rivals and establishes eunuch administration
events.description: The Yongle Emperor (Zhu Di), who had seized the throne in 1402 from his nephew, continued to consolidate power in 1404 by purging potential rivals and establishing a powerful eunuch administration. This period saw the rise of eunuchs like Zheng He to positions of significant authority. The Yongle Emperor's reliance on eunuchs as trusted administrators would shape Ming governance for decades and enable ambitious projects like the Forbidden City and the treasure voyages.
key_figures: Zheng He (eunuch administrator, rising in power)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)
Historiographic consensus on Yongle Emperor's reign

events.certainty_note: The Yongle Emperor's consolidation of power is well-documented in Ming administrative records, though specific purges and administrative changes in 1404 are not individually detailed.

events.title: Brunelleschi wins competition for Florence Cathedral dome; Renaissance architecture begins
events.description: Filippo Brunelleschi won the competition to design and construct the dome of the Florence Cathedral (Cattedrale di Santa Maria del Fiore), one of the most ambitious architectural projects of the Renaissance. The competition, held in 1401–1402, was formally awarded to Brunelleschi in 1404. His innovative engineering solution—using a double-shell dome without a wooden framework—would become a masterpiece of Renaissance engineering and a symbol of Florentine civic pride. Construction of the dome would continue until 1436.

sources.name: Vasari's Lives of the Artists (Vite de' più eccellenti pittori, scultori, e architettori)
Florentine archival records and guild documents

events.certainty_note: The competition and Brunelleschi's award are well-documented in Florentine archival records. Vasari's account, written in the 16th century, provides narrative context.

events.title: Venice and Ottoman Empire sign peace treaty; Mediterranean power balance
events.description: Venice and the Ottoman Empire signed a peace treaty in 1404, establishing a period of relative stability in the eastern Mediterranean. The treaty reflected the growing power of the Ottoman Empire and Venice's need to protect its commercial interests in the region. The agreement allowed both powers to focus on other priorities: Venice on its mainland Italian territories and the Ottomans on consolidating control in Anatolia and the Balkans.
key_figures: Venetian Republic (represented by doge and council)

sources.name: Venetian state archives and diplomatic records
Ottoman chronicles (Neşri, Ashikpashazade)

events.certainty_note: The treaty is attested in Venetian archival records, though Ottoman sources are less precise about the date. The treaty's terms and significance are well-documented in Venetian sources.

events.title: Zheng He appointed to military command; rise of eunuch power in Ming
events.description: Zheng He, a Muslim eunuch of Mongol descent, was appointed to a military command position in the Ming government, marking his rise to prominence. This appointment reflected the Yongle Emperor's trust in eunuch administrators and would eventually lead to Zheng He's appointment as admiral of the treasure fleet in 1405–1406. Zheng He's rise exemplified the Yongle Emperor's strategy of using eunuchs as counterweights to the traditional bureaucratic elite.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)
Biography of Zheng He in Ming historical records

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's early appointments are not precisely dated in surviving records. The chronology of his rise is inferred from later biographical accounts and administrative records.

disconfirming_evidence: Timur's planned campaign against Ming China is attested only in later Timurid sources (Zafarnama, written in the 16th century) and is not mentioned in contemporary Ming sources. This raises questions about the campaign's historical reality and significance. Some scholars argue that the campaign plan may have been exaggerated or invented by later Timurid historians to enhance Timur's legacy. The Venice-Ottoman treaty of 1404 is well-documented in Venetian sources but less precisely dated in Ottoman sources, suggesting possible discrepancies in dating or terms. Brunelleschi's competition award date is sometimes given as 1402 or 1403 in different sources, though 1404 is the most commonly accepted date.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1404 is moderate but uneven. Ming administrative records (Ming Shilu) provide good coverage of Chinese political events. Venetian archival records are abundant and contemporary. Italian Renaissance sources (Vasari, Florentine guild documents) are well-preserved. Timurid sources (Zafarnama) are later compilations, written in the 16th century, and may conflate or embellish events. Ottoman sources are also later compilations. The historical record is heavily skewed toward literate, centralized states (Ming China, Venice, Ottoman Empire, Italian city-states) and away from oral cultures and decentralized societies. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Northern Europe are essentially undocumented for this year.

Southeast Asia (limited written records; oral traditions not systematically documented)

events.title: Yongle Emperor establishes new capital at Beijing; begins Forbidden City planning
events.description: The Yongle Emperor announced his intention to move the Ming capital from Nanjing to Beijing, a strategic decision reflecting his focus on defending against Mongol threats from the north and projecting Ming power into Central Asia. In 1403, planning for the Forbidden City and other major Beijing projects began. The move represented a fundamental shift in Ming governance and would have profound implications for Chinese politics, military strategy, and cultural development. Beijing would remain the capital for the rest of the Ming Dynasty and beyond.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)
Historiographic consensus on Yongle Emperor's reign

events.certainty_note: The Yongle Emperor's decision to move the capital is well-documented in Ming administrative records. The exact date of the announcement is not always precisely specified, but 1403 is the conventional date for the formal decision.

events.title: Zheng He's early career in Ming military; preparation for future prominence
events.description: Zheng He, a Muslim eunuch of Mongol descent, was serving in the Ming military and beginning to gain prominence in the Yongle Emperor's administration. In 1403, Zheng He was likely involved in military campaigns or administrative duties, though specific events are not well-documented. His rise to prominence would accelerate in the following years, culminating in his appointment as admiral of the treasure fleet in 1405–1406.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)
Biography of Zheng He in Ming historical records

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's early career is not precisely documented in surviving records. The chronology of his rise is inferred from later biographical accounts and administrative records.

events.title: Ghiberti and Brunelleschi compete for Florence Cathedral dome commission
events.description: The competition for the commission to design and construct the dome of the Florence Cathedral (Cattedrale di Santa Maria del Fiore) was formally announced and conducted in 1401–1402, with the results becoming clear in 1403. Lorenzo Ghiberti and Filippo Brunelleschi were the primary competitors. Although Brunelleschi would ultimately win the commission (formally awarded in 1404), the competition itself was a watershed moment in Renaissance art and engineering, demonstrating the Florentine commitment to artistic innovation and civic pride.

sources.name: Vasari's Lives of the Artists (Vite de' più eccellenti pittori, scultori, e architettori)
Florentine archival records and guild documents

events.certainty_note: The competition is well-documented in Florentine archival records, though the exact dates of announcement and judging are sometimes given as 1401–1402 or 1402–1403 in different sources.

events.title: Ottoman Empire consolidates control in Anatolia; Mehmed I's early reign
events.description: The Ottoman Empire under Mehmed I (or his predecessor, depending on succession dating) was consolidating control over Anatolia and the Balkans. This period saw Ottoman expansion into regions previously dominated by Byzantine and Serbian influence. The Ottomans were establishing administrative structures and military garrisons to secure their territorial gains. Ottoman expansion in this period would eventually lead to the conquest of Constantinople in 1453.

sources.name: Ottoman chronicles (Neşri, Ashikpashazade)
Historiographic consensus on Ottoman expansion

events.certainty_note: Ottoman succession dates and specific events in 1403 are not precisely documented in surviving sources. Ottoman chronicles are later compilations, often written decades after events.

events.title: Timur's empire in transition; succession planning and internal conflicts
events.description: In 1403, Timur (Tamerlane) was in his final years of life (he would die in February 1405) and the Timurid Empire was beginning to show signs of internal strain. Timur's sons and grandsons were positioning themselves for succession, and regional governors were consolidating their power bases. The empire's vast territorial extent made unified governance difficult, and the succession crisis that would follow Timur's death was already becoming apparent.

sources.name: Zafarnama (Book of Victories) by Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi
Historiographic consensus on Timurid succession

events.certainty_note: Specific events in 1403 are not well-documented. The broader context of Timurid succession is attested in later sources (Zafarnama, written in the 16th century).

disconfirming_evidence: The Yongle Emperor's decision to move the capital to Beijing is sometimes dated to 1402 or 1404 in different sources, though 1403 is the most commonly accepted date. The Florence Cathedral dome competition is sometimes dated to 1401–1402 or 1402–1403, with results announced at different times. Ottoman succession dates are disputed; some sources place Mehmed I's accession in 1404 or 1405 rather than earlier. Timur's health and capacity to govern in 1403 are not well-documented, and the extent of his involvement in succession planning is unclear.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1403 is moderate but uneven. Ming administrative records (Ming Shilu) provide good coverage of Chinese political events. Florentine archival records are abundant and contemporary. Timurid and Ottoman sources are later compilations, written decades or centuries after events, making precise dating difficult. The historical record is heavily skewed toward literate, centralized states (Ming China, Ottoman Empire, Italian city-states) and away from oral cultures and decentralized societies. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Northern Europe are essentially undocumented for this year. The record for 1403 is particularly sparse compared to later years, reflecting the general decline in documentation as one moves backward in time.

graph_edges.note: Yongle Emperor's decision to move capital to Beijing in 1403 led to Forbidden City construction beginning in 1405–1406
Florence Cathedral dome competition in 1403 led to Brunelleschi's formal award in 1404
Timurid succession tensions in 1403 culminated in Timur's death and succession crisis in 1405

era_context: Early Ming Dynasty; the Yongle Emperor (Zhu Di) has just seized the throne from his nephew, the Jianwen Emperor, in a civil war (1402). Timur (Tamerlane) is in his final years of life (dies February 1405). The Ottoman Empire is consolidating control in Anatolia and the Balkans. The Italian Renaissance is beginning to accelerate; humanist scholarship and artistic innovation are emerging in Florence and other Italian city-states.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (minimal written documentation)
Mesoamerica and South America (Aztec and Inca records sparse for this year)
Northern Europe and Scandinavia (limited documentation outside major political centers)
Southeast Asia (limited written records; oral traditions not systematically documented)

events.title: Yongle Emperor seizes Ming throne; civil war ends
events.description: The Yongle Emperor (Zhu Di), a prince and military commander, successfully seized the Ming throne from his nephew, the Jianwen Emperor (Zhu Yunwen), in a civil war known as the Jingnan Rebellion (1399–1402). The Yongle Emperor's victory marked a turning point in Ming history. He would go on to become one of the most powerful and ambitious Ming emperors, commissioning the Forbidden City, launching Zheng He's treasure voyages, and expanding Ming territorial control. The Jianwen Emperor's fate remains uncertain; he may have died in the palace fire during the final siege of Nanjing, or he may have escaped and lived in hiding.
key_figures: Zhu Yuanzhang (Hongwu Emperor, founder of Ming Dynasty, deceased)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)
Historiographic consensus on the Jingnan Rebellion

events.certainty_note: The Yongle Emperor's seizure of the throne in 1402 is well-documented in Ming administrative records. The date is firmly established in the historical record.

events.title: Battle of Ankara; Timur defeats Ottoman Sultan Bayezid I
events.description: On July 28, 1402, Timur (Tamerlane) defeated Ottoman Sultan Bayezid I at the Battle of Ankara in a decisive military engagement. Timur's victory temporarily halted Ottoman expansion and resulted in the capture of Bayezid I, who died in captivity in 1403. The battle had profound consequences for the Ottoman Empire, triggering a succession crisis and a period of internal conflict known as the Interregnum (1402–1413). However, the Ottoman Empire would recover and eventually become one of the dominant powers in the eastern Mediterranean and beyond.

sources.name: Zafarnama (Book of Victories) by Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi
Ottoman chronicles (Neşri, Ashikpashazade)
Historiographic consensus on the Battle of Ankara

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Ankara is one of the most well-documented events of this period, attested in multiple contemporary and near-contemporary sources. The date July 28, 1402, is firmly established.

events.title: Jianwen Emperor's fate uncertain; possible escape or death in palace fire
events.description: During the final siege of Nanjing in 1402, the Jianwen Emperor's fate became uncertain. Ming sources report that he died in a palace fire during the siege, but some scholars and contemporary observers believed he may have escaped and lived in hiding for the rest of his life. The uncertainty surrounding the Jianwen Emperor's fate became a persistent mystery in Ming history, with some later accounts claiming he lived as a Buddhist monk. This ambiguity contributed to the Yongle Emperor's political vulnerability and may have motivated some of his later policies.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)
Later Ming historical accounts and biographies

events.certainty_note: The Jianwen Emperor's death in a palace fire is the official Ming account, but this is disputed by some scholars and contemporary observers. The uncertainty reflects the political sensitivity of the succession and the Yongle Emperor's need to establish his legitimacy.

events.title: Donatello born; future Renaissance sculptor
events.description: Donato di Niccolò Bardi, known as Donatello, was born in Florence in 1402 (or possibly 1386, depending on the source). Donatello would become one of the most influential sculptors of the Renaissance, pioneering new techniques in bronze casting and marble sculpture. His works, including the bronze David (completed c. 1406) and the Mary Magdalene, would define Renaissance sculpture and influence artists for centuries.

sources.name: Vasari's Lives of the Artists (Vite de' più eccellenti pittori, scultori, e architettori)
Florentine archival records

events.certainty_note: Donatello's birth date is traditionally given as 1402, but some sources place it as early as 1386. Vasari's account, written in the 16th century, is the primary narrative source, and it may contain errors or conflate dates.

events.title: Timur's final year; preparations for Central Asian campaigns
events.description: In 1402, Timur (Tamerlane) was in his final years of life (he would die in February 1405). Despite his advanced age (approximately 66 years old), Timur was still actively engaged in military planning and governance. The Battle of Ankara in July 1402 demonstrated his continued military prowess. In the months following the battle, Timur was planning further campaigns and consolidating his control over Central Asia and Persia.

sources.name: Zafarnama (Book of Victories) by Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi
Historiographic consensus on Timur's final years

events.certainty_note: Timur's activities in 1402 are attested in later Timurid sources (Zafarnama), but these sources were written in the 16th century and may contain errors or embellishments.

disconfirming_evidence: The Jianwen Emperor's death in a palace fire is the official Ming account, but this is disputed by some scholars and contemporary observers who believed he may have escaped. This uncertainty reflects the political sensitivity of the succession and the Yongle Emperor's need to establish his legitimacy. Donatello's birth date is disputed; some sources place it as early as 1386, which would make him 16 years old in 1402 rather than a newborn. The Battle of Ankara is well-documented, but some Ottoman sources give slightly different dates or emphasize different aspects of the battle's significance. Timur's health and capacity to govern in 1402 are not well-documented, and the extent of his involvement in military planning is unclear.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1402 is moderate to good. Ming administrative records (Ming Shilu) provide excellent coverage of the Yongle Emperor's seizure of the throne and the civil war. The Battle of Ankara is one of the most well-documented events of this period, attested in multiple sources. Florentine archival records are abundant for Italian Renaissance events. Timurid and Ottoman sources are later compilations, written decades or centuries after events, making precise dating difficult. The historical record is heavily skewed toward literate, centralized states (Ming China, Ottoman Empire, Timurid Empire, Italian city-states) and away from oral cultures and decentralized societies. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Northern Europe are essentially undocumented for this year. The record for 1402 is richer than for earlier years, reflecting the general improvement in documentation as one moves forward in time.

graph_edges.note: Yongle Emperor's seizure of the throne in 1402 led to his decision to move the capital to Beijing in 1403
Battle of Ankara in 1402 triggered the Ottoman Interregnum (1402–1413)
Jianwen Emperor's fate was determined during the Yongle Emperor's seizure of the throne
Donatello's birth in 1402 led to his creation of the bronze David in 1406

era_context: The early 15th century spans the late Medieval period in Europe, the height of the Ming Dynasty in China, the Ottoman consolidation in Anatolia and the Balkans, and the Timurid Renaissance in Central Asia and Persia. This is an era of significant political fragmentation in Europe (competing kingdoms, city-states, and principalities), maritime exploration beginning to accelerate, and major Islamic empires at their cultural and military peak.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources; oral traditions and archaeological evidence underrepresented in Western historiography)
Southeast Asian kingdoms (sources exist but are less densely covered in English-language scholarship)
Pacific and Oceanic societies (minimal written documentation)

events.title: Timur's Invasion of Syria and Sack of Damascus
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane), the Central Asian conqueror, invaded Syria in late 1400 and early 1401, culminating in the sack of Damascus in January 1401. The city was plundered, its population massacred or enslaved, and its great mosques and markets destroyed. This campaign was part of Timur's broader campaign to consolidate control over the Levant and demonstrate Timurid military supremacy. The destruction of Damascus was one of the most devastating urban catastrophes of the medieval Islamic world and marked the peak of Timurid territorial expansion before Timur's death in February 1405.
key_figures: Faraj al-Ashraf (Mamluk Sultan of Egypt, nominal overlord of Syria)

sources.name: Ibn Arabshah, Life of Timur (Aja'ib al-Maqdur fi Nawa'ib Timur)
Al-Maqrizi, Chronicle of Egypt (Kitab al-Suluk)
Yazdi, Zafarnama (Book of Victory)

events.certainty_note: The sack of Damascus in January 1401 is well-attested in multiple Islamic chronicles written within a century of the event. Timur's campaigns are extensively documented in Timurid and Mamluk sources.
cross_references: Follows from 1400-001 (Timur's invasion of Syria begins)

events.title: Zheng He's Early Naval Expeditions (Ming China)
events.description: Zheng He, the Muslim eunuch admiral of the Ming Dynasty, began his career in the early 1400s as a trusted commander under the Yongle Emperor. While his famous treasure voyages (Zheng He's voyages) are traditionally dated to begin in 1405, evidence suggests he was already commanding naval operations and building the massive treasure ships in 1401–1404. These expeditions were diplomatic and commercial missions designed to project Ming power across the Indian Ocean and establish tributary relationships with distant states. The scale of these voyages—with fleets of hundreds of ships and tens of thousands of crew—was unprecedented in world history.
key_figures: Zheng He (Admiral, Muslim eunuch of the Ming court)
Yongle Emperor (Zhu Di; Ming Dynasty ruler)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Luo Maodeng, Zheng He's Voyages (later compilation based on official records)

events.certainty_note: The exact start date of Zheng He's voyages is debated. Traditional sources cite 1405, but recent scholarship suggests preparations and early operations began in 1401–1404. The Ming Shilu provides contemporary documentation.
cross_references: Leads to 1405-001 (First official Zheng He voyage)

events.title: Joannes I of Portugal Consolidates Power; Portuguese Expansion Begins
events.description: João I (John I), founder of the House of Aviz, consolidated his rule over Portugal in the early 1400s following his victory at the Battle of Aljubarrota (1385). By 1401, his dynasty was secure, and he began laying the groundwork for Portuguese maritime expansion. His son, Prince Henry the Navigator, was born in 1394 and was already becoming influential in court. The Portuguese court began to sponsor exploration and trade missions along the African coast, which would eventually lead to the Age of Exploration. This period marks the beginning of European overseas expansion.
key_figures: João I (John I of Portugal; House of Aviz)
Prince Henry the Navigator (son of João I)

sources.name: Fernão Lopes, Chronicle of João I (Cronica de El-Rei Dom João I)
Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery of Guinea

events.certainty_note: João I's reign and the early Portuguese court are well-documented in Portuguese chronicles. The consolidation of his power by 1401 is established historical fact.

events.title: Owain Glyndŵr's Welsh Rebellion Continues
events.description: Owain Glyndŵr (Owen Glendower), a Welsh nobleman, led a major rebellion against English rule that began in 1400 and continued through the early 1400s. By 1401, Glyndŵr had secured significant support from Welsh commoners and nobility, and his forces controlled much of Wales. He declared himself Prince of Wales and sought to establish an independent Welsh state. The rebellion was ultimately suppressed by English forces under Henry IV, but it represented the last major Welsh uprising against English rule and became a symbol of Welsh national identity. The conflict lasted until approximately 1415.
key_figures: Owain Glyndŵr (Welsh rebel leader, self-proclaimed Prince of Wales)

sources.name: Adam of Usk, Chronicle
Iolo Goch, Welsh poetry (contemporary)

events.certainty_note: Owain Glyndŵr's rebellion is well-documented in English and Welsh sources from the period. The chronology of the rebellion is established.

events.title: Forbidden City Construction Begins (Ming China)
events.description: The Yongle Emperor commissioned the construction of the Forbidden City (the Imperial Palace) in Beijing in 1406, but planning and preparation began in 1401. The project involved the mobilization of hundreds of thousands of workers, including soldiers, peasants, and craftsmen. The construction of the Forbidden City was one of the largest architectural projects of the medieval world and took approximately 14 years to complete (1406–1420). The palace complex served as the residence of Chinese emperors for nearly 500 years and remains one of the most significant architectural achievements of the Ming Dynasty.
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (Zhu Di; Ming Dynasty ruler)
Cai Xin (architect and official overseeing construction)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
Archaeological and architectural records of the Forbidden City

events.certainty_note: Construction is traditionally dated to begin in 1406, but planning and preparation occurred in 1401. The Ming Shilu provides contemporary documentation of the project.
cross_references: Part of Yongle Emperor's Beijing projects 1401–1424

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Timur's sack of Damascus (1401-001) is sometimes overstated in Western historiography as marking the 'end of the medieval Islamic world,' when in fact the Mamluk Sultanate and Ottoman Empire continued to flourish. Zheng He's voyages (1401-002) are sometimes presented as evidence of Chinese maritime superiority that was 'abandoned,' but recent scholarship emphasizes that these were state-sponsored diplomatic missions, not commercial ventures, and their cessation was a deliberate policy choice, not a failure. Owain Glyndŵr's rebellion (1401-004) is sometimes romanticized as a nationalist uprising, but it was also driven by personal grievances and feudal disputes; its 'Welsh national' character is partly a later historiographic construction.
historiographic_note: The year 1401 is well-documented in European, Islamic, and Chinese sources, but with significant regional biases. European sources (English, Portuguese, Welsh chronicles) are abundant and detailed. Islamic sources (Mamluk and Timurid chronicles) are rich but often written decades after events. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) are contemporary and detailed. However, sources from sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas are sparse or absent from the historical record as preserved in major archives. The surviving sources reflect the interests of literate elites (courts, religious institutions, merchants) and likely underrepresent the experiences of common people, women, and non-elite groups.

graph_edges.note: Timur's death in 1405 ended the Timurid expansion; his successors fragmented his empire
Early naval preparations led to the first official Zheng He voyage in 1405
Portuguese consolidation under João I enabled the conquest of Ceuta in 1415
Owain Glyndŵr's rebellion continued until its suppression around 1415
Planning in 1401 led to construction beginning in 1406 and completion in 1420
